samples.title: deepcode / metagpt-custom/qwen3-coder-480b-a35b-instruct

project_context.introduction:
### Purpose
The goal is to provide structured input for DeepCode architecture generation, test design, and requirements tracing.

### Product Scope

DeepCode is an AI-assisted online programming platform based on large language models. It provides algorithm learning, code writing, code judging, problem creation, solution publication, forum discussion, and AI-assisted learning capabilities for students, algorithm enthusiasts, and programmers. The system integrates a secure judging mechanism, a communication and learning platform, and large language model interfaces.

This document uses the specification terminology for RUCM, Vue, Spring Boot, AI, LLM, UC, JudgeHost, JudgeCore, problems, solutions, discussions, submission records, and user roles.

### Intended Audience

This document is intended for DeepCode developers, testers, administrators, requirements reviewers, and later architecture generation and acceptance testing workflows. Target end users include students, algorithm enthusiasts, programmers, unregistered visitors, registered users, and administrators.
project_context.asr:
## 5. Non-Functional Requirements

| ID | Quality | Requirement | Metric / Acceptance | Priority | Verification |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| NFR-001 | Security | The system shall enforce HTTPS for all external communication. | All external entrances are accessed through HTTPS. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-002 | Security | The system shall use OAuth 2.0 or JWT for user identity authentication. | Protected interfaces require valid authentication credentials. | High | Test |
| NFR-003 | Security | The system shall perform permission authentication in back-end filters. | Login state and permission validation are completed before requests enter the business layer. | High | Test |
| NFR-004 | Security | The system shall strictly validate user-submitted data. | Front end and back end jointly validate password length, image size, sensitive words, and other inputs; SQL injection and XSS attacks are prevented. | High | Test |
| NFR-005 | Security | The system shall execute code judging in independent Docker containers and limit resources. | Judging processes are limited by CPU time, memory size, and other resources. | High | Test |
| NFR-006 | Security | JudgeCore shall restrict prohibited system calls from user code. | seccomp is used to restrict prohibited system calls. | High | Test |
| NFR-007 | Security | The system shall expose only necessary ports. | Firewall rules restrict access to unnecessary ports. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-008 | Privacy / Integrity | The system shall store sensitive information such as passwords in encrypted form. | Passwords and other sensitive information are not stored in plaintext in the database. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-009 | Reliability | The system shall execute transaction rollback when database operations fail. | Database operation failures in common processes trigger transaction rollback. | High | Test |
| NFR-010 | Reliability | The system shall handle exceptions when calling external AI services. | On network timeout or service unavailability, the system prompts users to retry and records logs. | Medium | Test |
| NFR-011 | Reliability | The system shall back up the database daily and store backups offsite. | Database backups are executed daily and backup copies are stored offsite. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-012 | Performance | The system shall distribute client requests through an Nginx reverse proxy. | Client requests are reverse-proxied by Nginx to back-end servers. | Medium | Inspection |
| NFR-013 | Performance | The system shall distribute static resources through CDN. | Images, CSS, and JS files are distributed through CDN. | Medium | Inspection |
| NFR-014 | Performance | Judging tasks shall enter a waiting queue when threads are insufficient, and return service busy when the queue is full. | When no available thread exists and the queue is not full, tasks are queued; when the queue is full, new tasks are not accepted and a busy prompt is returned. | High | Test |
| NFR-015 | Observability | The system shall collect infrastructure, application, and database monitoring metrics. | CPU, memory, disk I/O, interface response time, error rate, QPS, database query latency, and connection count are collected. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-016 | Observability | The system shall trigger alerts according to explicit thresholds. | Alerts are triggered when CPU or memory usage exceeds 80%, interface error rate exceeds 1%, or database query latency exceeds 15 seconds. | High | Test |
| NFR-017 | Observability | The system shall use specified monitoring and alerting tools. | Prometheus is used for collection, Grafana for display, and PagerDuty or DingTalk robot for alert delivery. | Medium | Inspection |
| NFR-018 | Maintainability | Database tables and fields shall use standardized names and keep meanings explicit. | Table names and field names are readable and semantically clear. | Medium | Inspection |
| NFR-019 | Data Integrity | Explicit relationships and foreign key constraints shall be set between database tables. | Related tables have foreign key constraints to ensure data consistency. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-020 | Performance / Data | The system shall reasonably design indexes according to business needs to improve query performance. | Appropriate indexes are created on key query fields. | Medium | Analysis |
| NFR-021 | Maintainability | Database design shall follow third normal form and make explainable redundancy tradeoffs when query efficiency requires it. | The data model reduces unnecessary redundancy, and redundant fields have query-performance justification. | Medium | Analysis |
| NFR-022 | Deployability | The system shall support automated build, containerized deployment, automatic scaling, and fast rollback. | GitLab CI/CD builds Docker images, pushes them to a private registry, and deploys, scales, and rolls back through Kubernetes. | Medium | Demonstration |

## 7. Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
| --- | --- |
| C-001 | The front end shall use technologies such as Vue 3, TypeScript, Vuex, vue-router, Ant Design Vue, CodeMirror, wangEditor, ECharts, Axios, Webpack, Vue CLI, and ESLint. |
| C-002 | The back end shall use technologies such as Spring Boot 3.1.2, JDK 21-LTS, Lombok, MyBatis-Plus, Swagger, JWT, SMTP, Interceptor, WebFlux, and thread pools. |
| C-003 | Data storage shall use MySQL 5.7.44, Redis 7.4.6, and MinIO. |
| C-004 | Front-end and back-end interaction shall use RESTful APIs. |
| C-005 | The judging service shall consist of JudgeHost and JudgeCore and be called by the back end through HTTP requests. |
| C-006 | Browser clients shall support HTML5 and be compatible with Microsoft Edge, Chrome, Opera, Safari, and Firefox. |
| C-007 | The production server shall use Ubuntu 22.04 LTS, at least 8 CPU cores, at least 32 GB memory, and at least 4 TB disk. |
| C-008 | The development and test environment shall use Ubuntu 22.04 LTS, at least 2 CPU cores, at least 8 GB memory, and at least 200 GB di
...[truncated]

completeness.reasoning: The diagram covers the majority of the system's components, including the front end, proxy, business modules, databases, caching, object storage, and observability tools (Prometheus, Grafana). However, it misses the explicitly required deployment and CI/CD components (GitLab, Docker, Kubernetes).

FR-008 specifies that JudgeHost calls JudgeCore, but the diagram shows the backend Judging Service calling both directly. Furthermore, the external LLM Service is depicted directly accessing internal databases and Redis caches, which contradicts the requirement that the backend calls the LLM service via LangChain4j.

Allowing an external LLM cloud service to directly access the internal data store is a critical security and architectural anti-pattern.

samples.title: prompthub / direct/gpt-5

project_context.introduction:
### Product Scope

PromptHub is a community Web application for sharing AI artworks and Prompts. The system scope includes AI artwork browsing and discussion, artwork categorization and retrieval, user artwork upload and administrator review, user profile and relationship management, favorite management, comments and replies, popular and personalized recommendations, notifications, and the front-end, back-end, algorithm support, and data persistence capabilities that support these functions.

This document follows the terminology definitions in the requirements specification for AIGC, Prompt, Vue.js, Django, SQLite, and Docker. The specification does not define API paths, database table structures, or concrete data exchange formats.

### Intended Audience

This document is intended for PromptHub developers, testers, project managers, requirements reviewers, later architecture generation workflows, and stakeholders who need to understand requirement coverage.
project_context.asr:
## 5. Non-Functional Requirements

| ID | Quality | Requirement | Fit Criterion | Priority | Verification |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| NFR-001 | Performance | Services such as account management, likes, and favorite management shall respond quickly. | Login, registration, likes, account management, and favorite management service response time does not exceed 0.5 seconds. | High | Test |
| NFR-002 | Performance | Image display shall respond quickly. | Each image display remains within 0.5 seconds. | High | Test |
| NFR-003 | Performance | For images that can be loaded as streams and do not need to be displayed all at once, the system may use a longer loading process. | The test report shall distinguish ordinary image display from streaming loading scenarios. | Medium | Analysis |
| NFR-004 | Reliability | The system single point of failure mean occurrence time shall satisfy the reliability threshold. | Under Docker deployment, mean time to single point failure is greater than 720 hours. | High | Analysis |
| NFR-005 | Reliability | The system shall limit data loss after failure. | Data lost after failure does not exceed 1%. | High | Test |
| NFR-006 | Reliability | The system shall automatically recover and notify developers after an error. | Docker automatically restarts and sends an email alert to developers. | High | Test |
| NFR-007 | Reliability | Persistent user data shall have offsite disaster recovery backups. | After user data is persistently stored, offsite disaster recovery backups exist. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-008 | Reliability | Server disk usage shall be controlled. | Server disk usage is below 80%; when the threshold is reached, operations staff are reminded to expand capacity. | High | Test |
| NFR-009 | Security | Web requests and user operations shall be protected. | Secure HTTP requests are used, and user operations are authenticated. | High | Test |
| NFR-010 | Security | The server shall prevent common Web attacks. | Firewall and cross-origin access control are enabled, and illegal links, XSS, and SQL injection are prohibited. | High | Test |
| NFR-011 | Security | Source dependencies shall undergo vulnerability checks. | Well-known vulnerability scanning tools are used to inspect source code, and packages with security vulnerabilities are prohibited. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-012 | Security | User sensitive data shall be encrypted and protected. | End-to-end encryption is used, and both server and client encrypt stored user sensitive data. | High | Test |
| NFR-013 | Security | Front-end publishing and back-end validation shall reduce malicious tampering risk. | The front end is published after obfuscation and source compression, and all data is revalidated on the back end. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-014 | Security | Internal personnel shall not disseminate source code or user data. | Developers must not disseminate source code or user data. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-015 | Maintainability | Project documents shall be clear, readable, and uniformly standardized. | Requirements, design, testing, and related documents pass standardization checks. | Medium | Inspection |
| NFR-016 | Maintainability | System logs shall support fault localization. | All logs are backed up and stored, and error occurrence time can be located from logs. | High | Test |
| NFR-017 | Maintainability | Product development shall be organized by modules. | Modules have high cohesion and low coupling and support later extension. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-018 | Usability | Interaction design shall reduce user operation cost. | Users can obtain target information in three clicks or fewer, most operations can be completed using only the mouse, and user habits can be recorded through cookies. | Medium | Demonstration |
| NFR-019 | UI Design | Interface design shall be unified, responsive, and information-hierarchical. | Style is unified, layout is responsive, information hierarchy is clear, icons are concise and intuitive, colors are consistent and harmonious, and text uses mature open-source fonts. | Medium | Inspection |

## 7. Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
| --- | --- |
| C-001 | Requirement changes must strictly follow the requirement change process, and the requirements specification must be reasonably modified and reviewed. |
| C-002 | The project development process must strictly follow the experiment plan. |
| C-003 | During runtime, the system needs to limit simultaneous access volume to ensure safe and stable operation. |
| C-004 | The front end is developed with HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and frameworks such as Vue and JQuery. |
| C-005 | The back end is developed with Python, Java, and the Django framework. |
| C-006 | The algorithm support module is developed with Python and frameworks such as PyTorch and TensorFlow. |
| C-007 | Because of runtime environment constraints, the back-end database uses SQLite; later high-concurrency scenarios may migrate to databases such as MySQL. |
| C-008 | Client operating systems support GUI operating systems based on Windows 7 and macOS 8.0 or above kernels. |
| C-009 | Client browsers must satisfy requirements such as Firefox 119.0 or above or Chromium 110.0 or above. |
| C-010 | When users cannot connect to the network, all requirements cannot be realized. |
| C-011 | Minimum client hardware is 8th Generation Intel Core i7, NVIDIA GeForce MX250, and 4 GB memory. |
| C-012 | Recommended server configuration is Intel Core i5-10600K or above, NVIDIA GeForce GTX 2060 or above, and 32 GB memory or above. |
| C-013 | Server software environment is Ubuntu 22.0.4 and Docker 20.10.17. |

### Operating Environment

| Environment | Requirement |
| --- | --- |
| Minimum client hardware | CPU: 8th Generation Intel Core i7; GPU: NVIDIA GeForce MX250; memory: 4 GB. |
| Recommended client hardware | CPU: Intel Core i5-10600K or above; GPU: NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1060 or above; memory: 16 GB or above. |
| Client software | Chrome 11
...[truncated]

samples.gt_source:
@startuml
' Configuration for layout and styling
skinparam linetype ortho
top to bottom direction

' Define Colors and Styles
skinparam rectangle {
    BackgroundColor<<frontend>> #e1f5fe
    BorderColor<<frontend>> #01579b
    
    BackgroundColor<<service>> #fff9c4
    BorderColor<<service>> #fbc02d
    
    BackgroundColor<<framework>> #ede7f6
    BorderColor<<framework>> #512da8
    
    BackgroundColor<<algo>> #f1f8e9
    BorderColor<<algo>> #558b2f
    
    BackgroundColor<<infra>> #e8eaf6
    BorderColor<<infra>> #3f51b5
    BorderStyle<<infra>> dashed
}

skinparam database {
    BackgroundColor #f3e5f5
    BorderColor #7b1fa2
}

' =========================
' Frontend Layer
' =========================
rectangle "Frontend / Client Layer" as Frontend <<frontend>> {
    rectangle "User System" as UserSys <<frontend>> {
        node "User Login" as UL
        node "View Home Page" as VHP
        node "Follow Others" as FO
        node "Browse History" as BH
    }

    rectangle "Admin System" as AdminSys <<frontend>> {
        node "Admin Login" as AL
        node "View User List" as VUL
        node "View Works List" as VWL
        node "Audit / Review Works" as ARW
    }

    rectangle "Display System" as DisplaySys <<frontend>> {
        node "Upload Works" as UW
        node "Query Works" as QW
        node "View Works" as VW
        node "Query Upload Progress" as QU
    }

    rectangle "Collection System" as CollectionSys <<frontend>> {
        node "Add Collection" as AC
        node "Collection Category" as CC
        node "Query Collection" as QC
        node "Edit Collection" as EC
    }

    rectangle "Comment System" as CommentSys <<frontend>> {
        node "Comment on Works" as CW
        node "Delete Comment" as DC
        node "Reply to Comment" as RC
        node "Delete Reply" as DR
    }

    rectangle "Notification System" as NotifySys <<frontend>> {
        node "Comment Notification" as CN
        node "Reply Notification" as RN
        node "Audit Notification" as AN
        node "Private Message - TBD" as PM
    }

    rectangle "Recommendation System" as RecommendSys <<frontend>> {
        node "Hot Recommendations" as HR
        node "Personalized Recommendations" as PR
        node "Close Recommendations" as OR
    }
}

' =========================
' Service / Backend Layer
' =========================
rectangle "Service / Backend Layer" as ServiceLayer <<service>> {
    
    rectangle "Backend Framework: Django" as Django <<framework>>

    rectangle "Business Services" as CoreServices <<service>> {
        node "Account Service" as AccountSvc
        node "Authentication Service" as AuthSvc
        node "Resource Service" as ResourceSvc
        node "Works Service" as WorksSvc
        node "Audit Service" as AuditSvc
        node "Notification Service" as NotifySvc
    }

    rectangle "Recommendation Algorithms" as RecommendAlgo <<algo>> {
        node "Content Heat / Popularity Based" as Algo1
        node "User Collection / History Based" as Algo2
        node "Social Relation Based" as Algo3
    }

    database "SQLite Database" as DB
}

' =========================
' Deployment Layer
' =========================
rectangle "Infrastructure" as Deployment <<infra>> {
    node "Docker Deployment" as Docker
}

' =========================
' Relationships
' =========================
Frontend --> CoreServices
CoreServices --> RecommendAlgo
RecommendAlgo --> Django
RecommendAlgo --> DB
ServiceLayer ..> Docker : "deploys to"

@enduml

samples.title: s000050_275af3e0 / metagpt-custom/gpt-5

project_context.introduction:
### Purpose
This SRS defines the software requirements for sample `s000050_275af3e0`, covering the repository’s supported fuzzing workflows, interfaces, data, constraints, and verification criteria.

### Product Scope
hAFL2 is a kAFL-based hypervisor fuzzing system. The primary supported scope includes fuzzing of the Hyper-V virtualization stack, especially the Hyper-V virtual switch (`vmswitch.sys`) path. The currently specified repository capabilities include:
- A standalone entry point for running `kAFL-Fuzzer` test suites
- A Linux userspace fuzzing workflow that packages target binaries into an `initrd`
- An embedded forkserver that maps kAFL hypercalls to AFL-style guest I/O
- Windows helper binaries used in the Hyper-V fuzzing workflow
- A harness-driver and crash-monitoring workflow used with child and root partition virtual machines

### Intended Audience
- Fuzzing operators using hAFL2 and kAFL workflows
- Developers maintaining test and launch workflows
- Integrators preparing Linux userspace or Windows Hyper-V fuzzing environments

### References
- Repository: `SafeBreach-Labs/hAFL2`
- Architecture documentation: `Architecture.png`
- Tutorial and workflow documentation: `tutorial.md`
project_context.asr:
## 5. Non-Functional Requirements

| ID | Quality Attribute | Requirement | Priority | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NFR-001 | Verifiability | The system shall support execution of regular `kAFL-Fuzzer` tests via `pytest` from the `kAFL-Fuzzer/` directory and via a standalone script entry point. | High | Test |
| NFR-002 | Data integrity | Deterministic mutation routines shall restore the original payload contents on exit, even when mutating the payload directly during processing. | High | Test |
| NFR-003 | Mutation correctness | Deterministic bitflip mutators shall produce outputs whose Hamming-distance change matches the mutator’s expected flipped-bit count under test. | High | Test |
| NFR-004 | Interoperability | The Windows fuzzing workflow shall operate across child partition and root partition virtual-machine roles. | High | Demonstration |
| NFR-005 | Configurability | The Windows workflow shall allow Windows-build-specific offset configuration when required for the target build. | Medium | Inspection |

## 7. System Constraints

| ID | Constraint | Type |
|---|---|---|
| C-001 | Use or redistribution shall comply with the repository-level BSD 3-Clause license and any applicable subfile license notices, including AGPL-3.0-or-later headers present in kAFL-derived files. | License |
| C-002 | Windows fuzzing binaries must be compiled by running `bash ./hAFL2/targets/windows_86_64/compile.sh`. | Build/Tooling |
| C-003 | The Windows harness and crash-monitoring drivers must be compiled with Visual Studio. | Build/Tooling |
| C-004 | For Windows builds different from the documented one, the operator must obtain the required offset and update the corresponding source value before compilation. | Platform compatibility |
| C-005 | Child Partition DSE must be disabled from an elevated command prompt, and the child partition VM must be restarted afterward. | Operational |
| C-006 | Child partition VM configuration steps require the child partition VM to be turned off before configuration begins. | Operational |

### Operating Environment
- Python 3 for the standalone test entry point
- `pytest` for regular test execution
- `bash` for build and launch-related scripts
- Linux environments for userspace target packaging and launch workflows
- Windows environments for helper binaries, harness, crash monitoring, and VM-based fuzzing setup
- Root partition and child partition virtual machines for the Hyper-V fuzzing workflow

### Software/API Interfaces
- The Linux userspace fuzzing workflow shall interface with guest execution through an embedded forkserver that maps kAFL hypercalls to AFL-style I/O.
- The Windows fuzzing workflow shall provide helper binaries that interact with the harness and snapshot-loading flow.
- The Windows Hyper-V fuzzing workflow shall support harness, crash-monitoring, and VM-based integration across child and root partitions.

### Communication Interfaces
- The Windows workflow shall support packet-sending through an IOCTL triggered by `packet_sender.exe`.
- The Windows Hyper-V fuzzing workflow shall support sending fuzzing payloads from the child partition harness to `VMSwitch` in the root partition.
- This communication path shall depend on netvsc or VMBus-related network channels.
- The crash-monitoring workflow shall communicate crash results back to hAFL2.

### Assumptions and Dependencies
- Linux userspace fuzzing assumes the target binary can be packaged into an `initrd`
- Shared libraries required by the target are available on the host for copying into the `initrd`
- Windows setup depends on compiling helper binaries and drivers and on Windows-build-specific offset configuration
- Hyper-V fuzzing depends on correct child partition and root partition VM setup
- The documented architecture and workflow assume VMBus or netvsc-related channels are available for the root-partition communication path

samples.title: modelevolution / mini-swe-agent/deepseek-v3.2

project_context.introduction:
### Product Scope

The Model Evolution Version Management Visualization Tool targets learners, contributors, and administrators of machine learning models. It visually displays model architecture, performance, and evolution relationships, and supports account management, model search and viewing, model upload and modification, model evolution relationship definition, and user management. The system especially targets models with multiple evolutionary versions, such as YOLO models, helping users understand model structures, source code, and evolution relationships.

### Intended Audience

This document is intended for developers, testers, system users, and later architecture generation/review workflows. The specification explicitly uses the requirements document as the basis for design, implementation, and testing phases, and provides users with operation and permission baselines.
project_context.asr:
## 5. Non-Functional Requirements

| ID | Quality | Requirement | Fit Criterion | Priority | Verification |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| NFR-001 | Concurrency | The system shall support concurrent online requests. | It can withstand simultaneous online requests from 50 users. | High | Test |
| NFR-002 | Throughput | The system shall meet throughput requirements for model modification and query requests. | 10 model modification requests per second and 50 query requests per second. | High | Test |
| NFR-003 | Response Time | Static pages shall respond quickly. | Static page response time does not exceed 1 second. | High | Test |
| NFR-004 | Processing Time | Adding a model to producing results shall be completed within the specified time. | Time from adding a model to producing results does not exceed 1 minute. | High | Test |
| NFR-005 | Reliability / Availability | The Web site shall run continuously without interruption. | Runs 7*24 hours, and unplanned downtime per year is no more than 30 hours. | High | Analysis |
| NFR-006 | Failover | The system shall quickly switch to a standby machine on failure. | Switches to a standby machine within 0.5 hours after failure. | High | Analysis |
| NFR-007 | Maintainability / Reliability | The system shall satisfy repairability and fault-free operation requirements. | Mean time to repair does not exceed 0.5 hours, and mean time between failures is no less than 4000 hours. | High | Analysis |
| NFR-008 | Consistency | When multiple administrators modify models simultaneously, the system shall keep final displayed content consistent. | No content conflict, confusion, or loss occurs. | High | Test |
| NFR-009 | Correctness | The system shall correctly display model structures and evolution relationships and return correct search results. | No exception occurs during user use; model structures and evolution relationships are displayed correctly; search returns correct results. | High | Test |
| NFR-010 | Authentication Security | User login shall use two-factor authentication. | OAuth 2.0 or a similar security protocol is used. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-011 | Data Encryption | Sensitive data shall be encrypted during transmission and storage. | TLS encryption is used and PCI DSS is satisfied. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-012 | Model Encryption | Models in the database shall be stored encrypted. | Symmetric encryption algorithms such as AES and DES are used. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-013 | Access Control | Before login, the system shall reject all actions that modify website content. | Before successful account login, users cannot upload/modify models or define evolution details. | High | Test |
| NFR-014 | Privilege Control | Non-administrator accounts shall not delete or modify any approved model. | Deletion/modification requests from non-administrators for approved models return rejection results. | High | Test |
| NFR-015 | Spoofing Protection | The system shall detect or block forged login data. | When accessed by an impersonated user, the system detects or blocks forged login data. | High | Test |
| NFR-016 | Tamper Protection | The system shall protect uploaded models and evolution details from arbitrary tampering or deletion. | Unauthorized tampering or deletion operations are rejected and do not affect data integrity. | High | Test |
| NFR-017 | Privacy Compliance | The system shall follow local privacy regulations to protect personal data. | A privacy policy is provided and user consent is obtained. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-018 | License Compliance | Third-party libraries, plugins, and tools shall comply with open-source licenses. | They do not infringe others' intellectual property rights. | Medium | Inspection |
| NFR-019 | Maintainability | When a system fault occurs, it shall be possible to quickly locate and resolve the problem. | Fault location and resolution processes are executable; code is easy to understand, modify, and enhance. | Medium | Inspection |
| NFR-020 | Code Consistency | Code in the same module shall be written in the same format. | Variable names are semantically related, structure and presentation are separated, HTML is used for structure, and CSS is used for layout and presentation. | Medium | Inspection |

| NFR-022 | Usability | Users shall clearly understand website functions and complete tasks according to prompts. | New users can complete tasks according to prompts; experienced users can complete tasks quickly and efficiently; detailed help documents are provided. | Medium | Demonstration |
| NFR-023 | UI Aesthetics | System pages shall be coordinated, prominent, and easy to understand. | Navigation interfaces do not exceed 10; the main interface is prominent, side and bottom columns are clear, and long pages have bottom navigation. | Medium | Inspection |

## 7. Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
| --- | --- |
| C-001 | The system adopts a B/S architecture, and users access a specific website through a browser. |
| C-002 | The hardware environment shall satisfy x86-64 or ARM CPU, at least 4 GB memory, at least 20 GB available disk, and at least 5 Mbps bandwidth. |
| C-003 | The operating system environment shall support Windows 10 or above 64-bit or Ubuntu 16.04 or above 64-bit, and satisfy cross-platform constraints such as Windows, macOS, and Linux. |
| C-004 | The runtime environment includes mainstream browsers such as Firefox, Chrome, and 360 Browser. |
| C-005 | There are no hardware interfaces. |
| C-006 | Software interfaces include Python libraries and browser Console. |
| C-007 | Communication interfaces include HTTP, HTTPS, TCP, IP, FTP, SMTP, and UDP. |
| C-008 | The databases are N

@startuml
' Directional setting (Top to Down)
top to bottom direction

' Styling for different layers
skinparam rectangle {
    BackgroundColor<<Frontend>> #e1f5fe
    BorderColor<<Frontend>> #01579b
    
    BackgroundColor<<Presentation>> #f3e5f5
    BorderColor<<Presentation>> #4a148c
    
    BackgroundColor<<Backend>> #fff3e0
    BorderColor<<Backend>> #e65100
}

skinparam database {
    BackgroundColor<<Storage>> #e8f5e9
    BorderColor<<Storage>> #1b5e20
}

' Layers / Subgraphs
package "Frontend Layer" {
    rectangle "React Framework" as React <<Frontend>>
    rectangle "React-Router: Page Navigation" as Router <<Frontend>>
    rectangle "Netron: Node Drawing Features" as NetronFE <<Frontend>>
}

package "Data Presentation Layer" {
    rectangle "RESTful API Design" as RESTAPI <<Presentation>>
    rectangle "Netron: Graph Representation Language" as GraphLang <<Presentation>>
}

package "Backend Layer" {
    rectangle "Flask: Backend Framework" as Flask <<Backend>>
    rectangle "SQLAlchemy: ORM Framework" as SQLAlchemy <<Backend>>
    rectangle "Netron: Model Conversion & Parsing" as NetronBE <<Backend>>
}

package "Storage / Database Layer" {
    database "MySQL: Relational Database" as MySQL <<Storage>>
    database "Neo4j: Graph Database" as Neo4j <<Storage>>
}

' Relationships
React --> Router
React --> NetronFE
React --> RESTAPI
NetronFE --> GraphLang
RESTAPI --> Flask
GraphLang --> Flask
Flask --> SQLAlchemy
Flask --> NetronBE
SQLAlchemy --> MySQL
Flask --> Neo4j

@enduml

samples.title: deepcode / openhands/qwen3-coder-480b-a35b-instruct

project_context.introduction:
### Purpose
The goal is to provide structured input for DeepCode architecture generation, test design, and requirements tracing.

### Product Scope

DeepCode is an AI-assisted online programming platform based on large language models. It provides algorithm learning, code writing, code judging, problem creation, solution publication, forum discussion, and AI-assisted learning capabilities for students, algorithm enthusiasts, and programmers. The system integrates a secure judging mechanism, a communication and learning platform, and large language model interfaces.

This document uses the specification terminology for RUCM, Vue, Spring Boot, AI, LLM, UC, JudgeHost, JudgeCore, problems, solutions, discussions, submission records, and user roles.

### Intended Audience

This document is intended for DeepCode developers, testers, administrators, requirements reviewers, and later architecture generation and acceptance testing workflows. Target end users include students, algorithm enthusiasts, programmers, unregistered visitors, registered users, and administrators.
project_context.asr:
## 5. Non-Functional Requirements

| ID | Quality | Requirement | Metric / Acceptance | Priority | Verification |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| NFR-001 | Security | The system shall enforce HTTPS for all external communication. | All external entrances are accessed through HTTPS. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-002 | Security | The system shall use OAuth 2.0 or JWT for user identity authentication. | Protected interfaces require valid authentication credentials. | High | Test |
| NFR-003 | Security | The system shall perform permission authentication in back-end filters. | Login state and permission validation are completed before requests enter the business layer. | High | Test |
| NFR-004 | Security | The system shall strictly validate user-submitted data. | Front end and back end jointly validate password length, image size, sensitive words, and other inputs; SQL injection and XSS attacks are prevented. | High | Test |
| NFR-005 | Security | The system shall execute code judging in independent Docker containers and limit resources. | Judging processes are limited by CPU time, memory size, and other resources. | High | Test |
| NFR-006 | Security | JudgeCore shall restrict prohibited system calls from user code. | seccomp is used to restrict prohibited system calls. | High | Test |
| NFR-007 | Security | The system shall expose only necessary ports. | Firewall rules restrict access to unnecessary ports. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-008 | Privacy / Integrity | The system shall store sensitive information such as passwords in encrypted form. | Passwords and other sensitive information are not stored in plaintext in the database. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-009 | Reliability | The system shall execute transaction rollback when database operations fail. | Database operation failures in common processes trigger transaction rollback. | High | Test |
| NFR-010 | Reliability | The system shall handle exceptions when calling external AI services. | On network timeout or service unavailability, the system prompts users to retry and records logs. | Medium | Test |
| NFR-011 | Reliability | The system shall back up the database daily and store backups offsite. | Database backups are executed daily and backup copies are stored offsite. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-012 | Performance | The system shall distribute client requests through an Nginx reverse proxy. | Client requests are reverse-proxied by Nginx to back-end servers. | Medium | Inspection |
| NFR-013 | Performance | The system shall distribute static resources through CDN. | Images, CSS, and JS files are distributed through CDN. | Medium | Inspection |
| NFR-014 | Performance | Judging tasks shall enter a waiting queue when threads are insufficient, and return service busy when the queue is full. | When no available thread exists and the queue is not full, tasks are queued; when the queue is full, new tasks are not accepted and a busy prompt is returned. | High | Test |
| NFR-015 | Observability | The system shall collect infrastructure, application, and database monitoring metrics. | CPU, memory, disk I/O, interface response time, error rate, QPS, database query latency, and connection count are collected. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-016 | Observability | The system shall trigger alerts according to explicit thresholds. | Alerts are triggered when CPU or memory usage exceeds 80%, interface error rate exceeds 1%, or database query latency exceeds 15 seconds. | High | Test |
| NFR-017 | Observability | The system shall use specified monitoring and alerting tools. | Prometheus is used for collection, Grafana for display, and PagerDuty or DingTalk robot for alert delivery. | Medium | Inspection |
| NFR-018 | Maintainability | Database tables and fields shall use standardized names and keep meanings explicit. | Table names and field names are readable and semantically clear. | Medium | Inspection |
| NFR-019 | Data Integrity | Explicit relationships and foreign key constraints shall be set between database tables. | Related tables have foreign key constraints to ensure data consistency. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-020 | Performance / Data | The system shall reasonably design indexes according to business needs to improve query performance. | Appropriate indexes are created on key query fields. | Medium | Analysis |
| NFR-021 | Maintainability | Database design shall follow third normal form and make explainable redundancy tradeoffs when query efficiency requires it. | The data model reduces unnecessary redundancy, and redundant fields have query-performance justification. | Medium | Analysis |
| NFR-022 | Deployability | The system shall support automated build, containerized deployment, automatic scaling, and fast rollback. | GitLab CI/CD builds Docker images, pushes them to a private registry, and deploys, scales, and rolls back through Kubernetes. | Medium | Demonstration |

## 7. Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
| --- | --- |
| C-001 | The front end shall use technologies such as Vue 3, TypeScript, Vuex, vue-router, Ant Design Vue, CodeMirror, wangEditor, ECharts, Axios, Webpack, Vue CLI, and ESLint. |
| C-002 | The back end shall use technologies such as Spring Boot 3.1.2, JDK 21-LTS, Lombok, MyBatis-Plus, Swagger, JWT, SMTP, Interceptor, WebFlux, and thread pools. |
| C-003 | Data storage shall use MySQL 5.7.44, Redis 7.4.6, and MinIO. |
| C-004 | Front-end and back-end interaction shall use RESTful APIs. |
| C-005 | The judging service shall consist of JudgeHost and JudgeCore and be called by the back end through HTTP requests. |
| C-006 | Browser clients shall support HTML5 and be compatible with Microsoft Edge, Chrome, Opera, Safari, and Firefox. |
| C-007 | The production server shall use Ubuntu 22.04 LTS, at least 8 CPU cores, at least 32 GB memory, and at least 4 TB disk. |
| C-008 | The development and test environment shall use Ubuntu 22.04 LTS, at least 2 CPU cores, at least 8 GB memory, and at least 200 GB di
...[truncated]

It accurately represents the front-end/back-end separation, the five architectural layers (Presentation, Proxy, Business, Access), the backend modules (User, Problem, Solution, Forum, AI), and all specified supporting technologies (Vue, Spring Boot, MySQL, Redis, MinIO, LLM, JudgeHost/Core, Nginx, CDN, Prometheus, Grafana, Swagger, LangChain4j, SMTP, JWT/OAuth2, MyBatis-Plus). Key non-functional requirements, especially Architecturally Significant Requirements (ASRs) like security, performance, and observability, are addressed with dedicated components or explicit notes.

faithfulness.reasoning: The architecture diagram is remarkably faithful to the SRS. Every component, technology, and relationship depicted directly maps to an explicit statement or a strong implicit inference from the requirements. There are no contradictions or misinterpretations of the SRS content. The diagram precisely reflects the 'Product Perspective' section regarding layering and module breakdown, and accurately integrates all specified external interfaces and constraints.

architectural_rationality.reasoning: The architectural design is highly rational and well-justified by the requirements. The adoption of a layered architecture, reverse proxy (Nginx), CDN for static assets, dedicated services for AI and judging, caching (Redis), object storage (MinIO), and a comprehensive observability stack (Prometheus, Grafana, Alerting) are all sound architectural decisions. These choices directly address the functional requirements and effectively mitigate critical non-functional requirements such as security (HTTPS, authentication filters, sandboxed judging), performance (Nginx, CDN, Redis, judging queue), reliability (implied by robust data stores and external service handling), and maintainability (clear separation of concerns). The design demonstrates a strong understanding of best practices for a system of this nature.

samples.title: mytorch / mini-swe-agent/qwen3-coder-480b-a35b-instruct

project_context.introduction:
### Product Scope

The MyTorch project targets deep learning learners. By continuously improving and extending the MyTorch/NNE framework, it provides framework enhancement capabilities and a visual training/inference system. The system scope includes model configuration, data loading, model training, model inference, GPU acceleration, model weight initialization, classic model introduction, preset datasets, back-end interfaces, front-end interfaces, and local database persistence.

This document follows the terminology definitions in the requirements specification for deep learning, neural networks, data augmentation, data loaders, model structures, loss functions, optimizers, learning rates, weight initialization, GPU acceleration, batches, and model deployment.

### Intended Audience

This document is intended for developers, team members, teachers, classmates, project stakeholders, and later architecture generation and test review workflows. The specification particularly emphasizes unifying understanding during development and testing, communicating requirements, and evaluating project progress.
project_context.asr:
## 5. Non-Functional Requirements

| ID | Quality | Requirement | Fit Criterion | Priority | Verification |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| NFR-001 | Numerical Precision | System floating-point calculation precision shall meet the specified standard. | All floating-point calculation precision is the same as or higher than `numpy.float32`, with at least 7 decimal significant digits. | High | Test |
| NFR-002 | Time Precision | System time-related records shall reach second-level precision. | Log time and other time-related precision is accurate to the second. | Medium | Inspection |
| NFR-003 | UI Response Time | On recommended hardware configuration, the interaction interface shall respond promptly to user instructions. | Maximum response time is less than 1 second, and average response time is less than 0.75 seconds. | High | Test |
| NFR-004 | Training Engine Response Time | On recommended hardware configuration, the training engine shall respond promptly to training or data-loading instructions. | Maximum response time is less than 3 seconds, and average response time is less than 2.5 seconds. | High | Test |
| NFR-005 | Training Efficiency | System training time shall improve with device performance and GPU acceleration capability. | When processor performance is higher or higher-performance GPU acceleration is used for training, the system shall have correspondingly higher training efficiency. | Medium | Analysis |
| NFR-006 | Device Variability | Under non-recommended hardware configuration, system response time may vary with device performance. | The test report shall explain that response time under non-recommended configuration is related to device performance. | Low | Analysis |
| NFR-007 | UI Intuitiveness | The interface shall be as simple and intuitive as possible to reduce the learning curve for beginners. | Clear icons, symbols, and workflows are used, and functional areas are reasonably laid out. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-008 | Guidance and Help | The interface shall provide step guidance, prompts, examples, and suggestions. | Users can view explanations of components and parameter roles, as well as help needed to build neural networks. | High | Demonstration |
| NFR-009 | Real-Time Feedback | The interface shall provide real-time feedback and detailed visualization. | After parameter changes or component additions, the interface updates immediately; when waiting for framework response, it shows a loading animation or progress bar. | High | Demonstration |
| NFR-010 | UI Consistency | Interface design shall remain consistent and concise. | Colors, fonts, buttons, and icon styles are consistent, reducing visual fatigue. | Medium | Inspection |
| NFR-011 | Fault Monitoring | The system shall monitor training and inference runtime exceptions. | It monitors exceptions such as gradient explosion and inference memory overflow. | High | Test |
| NFR-012 | Resource Monitoring | The system shall monitor key performance indicators. | It monitors GPU utilization, memory usage, and training speed. | High | Test |
| NFR-013 | Fault Handling | The system shall define fault handling strategies. | Training faults such as gradient explosion shall trigger strategies such as automatic training termination. | High | Test |
| NFR-014 | Recovery | The system shall support model backup and automatic recovery mechanisms. | When hardware failure occurs, model availability can be guaranteed through backup and recovery mechanisms. | High | Test |
| NFR-015 | Error Reporting | The system shall record and report error information and provide troubleshooting suggestions. | Users can understand problems and perform troubleshooting. | Medium | Demonstration |
| NFR-016 | Security and Privacy | The system shall protect user data and models from potential security threats. | Security and privacy protection mechanisms are adopted to prevent potential vulnerabilities or attacks. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-017 | Confidentiality and Integrity | The system shall ensure confidentiality and integrity of user data. | Unauthorized access or tampering shall not compromise user data and models. | High | Test |
| NFR-018 | Privacy Compliance | The system shall comply with relevant privacy regulations and standards. | Privacy protection design conforms to applicable regulations and standards. | High | Inspection |

## 7. Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
| --- | --- |
| C-001 | The project enhances the existing NNE framework code. |
| C-002 | Project development and testing shall be performed on computer devices that support deep learning tasks and include at least one computer with a GPU. |
| C-003 | The project uses publicly available datasets or datasets satisfying course requirements for development and testing. |
| C-004 | Project development progress and delivery are constrained by the course semester end date. |
| C-005 | Developers shall have basic deep learning, programming, teamwork, and communication skills. |
| C-006 | The supporting software environment is Python 3.8.x or above with corresponding libraries. |
| C-007 | Concrete library versions and configuration shall be listed in `requirements.txt`. |
| C-008 | The database is SQLite and is deployed in the user's local storage space with the system. |
| C-009 | The system shall automatically back up the SQLite database and support automatic recovery on exception and manual recovery by users. |
| C-010 | All floating-point computation precision must be no lower than `numpy.float32`. |
| C-011 | Time precision shall be accurate to the second. |
| C-012 | System security design shall comply with relevant privacy regulations and standards. |
| C-013 | This document references GB/T 8567-1988 Computer Software Requirements Specification. |

### Operating Environment

| Environment | Requirement |
| --- | --- |
| Development and test device | Computer devices that support deep learning tasks, including at least one computer with a GPU
...[truncated]

completeness.reasoning: The diagram successfully represents the main functional components described in the requirements, including the frontend/backend separation, framework extension, and GPU acceleration. However, it completely omits components or mechanisms to address several critical non-functional requirements that have architectural implications, such as database backup/recovery (FR-021, NFR-014), fault monitoring/handling (NFR-011, NFR-013), and security (NFR-016).

faithfulness.reasoning: The diagram includes a major unsupported inference that represents a significant architectural flaw. It depicts low-level framework components like 'Loss Functions' and 'Optimizers' connecting directly to the 'SQLite Database'. The requirements state that the system should persist logs and models (FR-020), which implies this logic should be in the application/backend layer (e.g., 'Training Engine'), not in the core framework components themselves. This violates separation of concerns and is not supported by the SRS.

architectural_rationality.reasoning: The architecture fails to propose a sound solution for key architectural drivers. While it correctly identifies components for performance (GPU Acceleration) and extensibility (Framework Extension), the data persistence strategy is fundamentally flawed. Having core mathematical components write directly to a database creates tight coupling and poor modularity. Furthermore, the architecture completely ignores specified ASRs for reliability (no backup/recovery mechanism shown despite being a high-priority requirement) and security.

traceability.reasoning: Most high-level components are easily traceable to the requirements. For example, the 'Frontend' components map to the 'User Interfaces' section, and 'GPU Acceleration' maps to FR-010-015. However, traceability breaks down for key relationships. The multiple connections from framework-level components to the database are a misinterpretation of the requirement for the *system* to persist data (FR-020), making these specific connections untraceable to a direct and correctly interpreted requirement.

readability.reasoning: The diagram is well-structured, using logical packages for layers (Frontend, Backend, Framework) and standard UML component notation. Component names are clear and map intuitively to the problem domain. The use of notes provides helpful context. samples.title: s000008_a5828142 / metagpt-custom/claude-sonnet-4-6

project_context.introduction:
### 1.1 Purpose
This SRS defines the final checked requirements for Bunny Code, a collaborative coding website with project workspaces, code execution, coding-process replay, real-time battle workflows, authentication, file distribution, and deployment infrastructure. It is prepared as a clean requirements input for downstream architecture diagram generation.

### 1.2 Product Scope
Bunny Code supports:
- User registration, login, JWT authentication, and user detail query.
- Project creation, query, update, version management, and initial file creation.
- Browser-based code editing and workspace authorization.
- Code compilation or execution with returned execution results.
- Recording and playback of coding processes by project, version, and time range.
- Real-time workspace collaboration using socket channels.
- Real-time battle invitation, battle acceptance, readiness, code synchronization, judging, ending, and leave handling.
- Upload and distribution of code files, battle result files, user files, static assets, and archive files.
- Backend coordination through Node.js/Express, REST APIs, Socket.IO channels, MySQL, InfluxDB, Redis, Docker-based code execution, S3-compatible object storage, CloudFront-style distribution, Nginx reverse proxying, and automated deployment flow.

### 1.3 Intended Audience
- Developers implementing frontend, backend, socket, storage, and deployment components.
- Testers validating authentication, project workflows, code execution, replay, battles, and file handling.
- Integrators connecting Bunny Code to databases, cache, object storage, CDN, sandbox, and deployment environments.
- Architecture analysts generating architecture diagrams from the checked requirements.

### 1.4 Terminology
| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| Workspace | A live project/version editing session accessed by a socket client. |
| Project | A coding workspace entity owned or accessed by users and composed of versions and files. |
| Version | A project revision used for editing, execution, recording, and playback. |
| Battle | A real-time coding competition between users with invitations, readiness, synchronized code, judging, and outcome handling. |
| Battle invitation | A temporary cache-backed invitation created by an inviter and accepted by another user. |
| Coding-process record | Time-series editing data used to replay a user's coding process. |
| Object storage | External storage used for code files, archives, battle files, user files, and distributable URLs. |
project_context.asr:
## 5. Non-Functional Requirements

| ID | Requirement | Quality Attribute | Priority | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NFR-001 | The battle invitation acceptance flow shall use Redis isolated execution and watched cache state to control concurrent access, so that the same temporary invitation can be consumed by at most one acceptance flow under concurrent acceptance. | Reliability / concurrency control | High | Inspection and concurrent acceptance test |
| NFR-002 | Cache-dependent workspace and battle operations shall verify Redis readiness before executing cache operations. | Availability dependency | High | Inspection |
| NFR-003 | Authentication-protected APIs and socket workflows shall require a valid authenticated user context before performing user-specific operations. | Security | High | Test |
| NFR-004 | Project workspace authorization shall preserve read-only behavior for unauthorized, incomplete, or missing project/version requests. | Access control | High | Test |
| NFR-005 | Code execution shall run through an isolated sandbox environment rather than directly inside the main backend process. | Safety / isolation | High | Inspection |
| NFR-006 | Coding-process playback data shall be retrievable by project, version, and time range. | Usability / data retrievability | Medium | Test |
| NFR-007 | Uploaded files and archive outputs shall be retrievable through distributable URLs after successful storage. | Interoperability | Medium | Demonstration |
| NFR-008 | Automated deployment shall support the configured frontend, backend, reverse proxy, cache, databases, sandbox, object storage, and distribution services. | Deployability | Medium | Inspection |

## 7. System Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
|---|---|
| C-001 | The backend shall run in a Node.js/Express server environment with Socket.IO support. |
| C-002 | The frontend shall run as a Vue browser application. |
| C-003 | Relational data shall be stored in MySQL. |
| C-004 | Coding-process replay data shall be stored in InfluxDB or an equivalent time-series store configured for the deployment. |
| C-005 | Live workspace and battle coordination shall depend on Redis readiness. |
| C-006 | Battle acceptance shall use Redis isolated execution with watched read/delete semantics for temporary invitation consumption. |
| C-007 | Redis connectivity shall be configured through deployment environment values for host, port, username, and password. |
| C-008 | Redis persistence, checksum, append-only, protected mode, and default user password settings are deployment configuration choices and shall not be treated as mandatory product-level behavior unless adopted by the target deployment. |
| C-009 | Code compilation or execution shall be performed through a Docker-based sandbox. |
| C-010 | Code files, battle result files, user files, static assets, and archive files shall be stored through object storage and distributed through configured URLs. |
| C-011 | Nginx reverse proxying and automated deployment scripts shall align with the configured frontend, backend, cache, databases, sandbox, object storage, and CDN endpoints. |

### 2.4 Operating Environment
- Browser frontend runtime for the Vue application.
- Node.js server runtime for Express and Socket.IO services.
- REST API and Socket.IO communication between frontend and backend.
- MySQL for relational application data.
- InfluxDB for time-series coding-process records.
- Redis for live workspace and battle coordination.
- Docker-based sandbox for code compilation or execution.
- S3-compatible object storage and CDN distribution for files and archives.
- Nginx reverse proxy and automated deployment environment.

### 3.3 Communication Interfaces
| ID | Interface | Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| COM-001 | REST communication | The frontend and backend shall exchange request/response data over REST-style HTTP APIs. |
| COM-002 | Socket.IO communication | The frontend and backend shall use real-time socket events for workspaces and battles. |
| COM-003 | Redis communication | The backend shall use Redis cache operations for live workspace coordination and battle state. |
| COM-004 | Database communication | The backend shall communicate with MySQL for relational data and InfluxDB for time-series records. |
| COM-005 | Sandbox communication | The backend shall invoke a Docker-based execution environment to compile or execute submitted code. |
| COM-006 | Object storage and CDN communication | The backend shall upload files to object storage and provide URLs suitable for distribution. |

### 2.5 Assumptions and Dependencies
- JWT authentication depends on a configured token signing and validation mechanism.
- Workspace and battle operations that use cache depend on Redis readiness.
- Code execution depends on an available sandbox environment.
- Process replay depends on persisted time-series coding records.
- File distribution depends on available object storage and URL distribution configuration.
- Final Redis persistence, checksum, append-only, protected mode, and authentication details are deployment configuration decisions rather than mandatory product behavior unless selected for the target deployment.

completeness.reasoning: The diagram represents all major components specified in the SRS, including the Vue frontend, Nginx proxy, Node.js/Express backend (decomposed into logical services like ProjVersionMgr and CodeExecSvc), Socket.IO handlers, Docker sandbox, and all specified data stores (MySQL, InfluxDB, Redis, S3/CDN). It also captures the key interactions between these components, resulting in a comprehensive view of the system.

faithfulness.reasoning: The architecture diagram is highly faithful to the requirements, accurately depicting component responsibilities and interactions without contradiction. For example, the separation of code execution into a Docker sandbox (NFR-005, C-009), the use of Redis for real-time state and concurrency control (NFR-001, C-006), and the use of InfluxDB for time-series data (C-004) are all correctly represented as described in the SRS.

All architectural choices are well-justified by the requirements.

All architecturally significant requirements, including all NFRs and Constraints, are clearly mapped to architectural elements.

samples.title: modelevolution / mini-swe-agent/gpt-5

project_context.introduction:
### Product Scope

The Model Evolution Version Management Visualization Tool targets learners, contributors, and administrators of machine learning models. It visually displays model architecture, performance, and evolution relationships, and supports account management, model search and viewing, model upload and modification, model evolution relationship definition, and user management. The system especially targets models with multiple evolutionary versions, such as YOLO models, helping users understand model structures, source code, and evolution relationships.

### Intended Audience

This document is intended for developers, testers, system users, and later architecture generation/review workflows. The specification explicitly uses the requirements document as the basis for design, implementation, and testing phases, and provides users with operation and permission baselines.
project_context.asr:
## 5. Non-Functional Requirements

| ID | Quality | Requirement | Fit Criterion | Priority | Verification |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| NFR-001 | Concurrency | The system shall support concurrent online requests. | It can withstand simultaneous online requests from 50 users. | High | Test |
| NFR-002 | Throughput | The system shall meet throughput requirements for model modification and query requests. | 10 model modification requests per second and 50 query requests per second. | High | Test |
| NFR-003 | Response Time | Static pages shall respond quickly. | Static page response time does not exceed 1 second. | High | Test |
| NFR-004 | Processing Time | Adding a model to producing results shall be completed within the specified time. | Time from adding a model to producing results does not exceed 1 minute. | High | Test |
| NFR-005 | Reliability / Availability | The Web site shall run continuously without interruption. | Runs 7*24 hours, and unplanned downtime per year is no more than 30 hours. | High | Analysis |
| NFR-006 | Failover | The system shall quickly switch to a standby machine on failure. | Switches to a standby machine within 0.5 hours after failure. | High | Analysis |
| NFR-007 | Maintainability / Reliability | The system shall satisfy repairability and fault-free operation requirements. | Mean time to repair does not exceed 0.5 hours, and mean time between failures is no less than 4000 hours. | High | Analysis |
| NFR-008 | Consistency | When multiple administrators modify models simultaneously, the system shall keep final displayed content consistent. | No content conflict, confusion, or loss occurs. | High | Test |
| NFR-009 | Correctness | The system shall correctly display model structures and evolution relationships and return correct search results. | No exception occurs during user use; model structures and evolution relationships are displayed correctly; search returns correct results. | High | Test |
| NFR-010 | Authentication Security | User login shall use two-factor authentication. | OAuth 2.0 or a similar security protocol is used. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-011 | Data Encryption | Sensitive data shall be encrypted during transmission and storage. | TLS encryption is used and PCI DSS is satisfied. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-012 | Model Encryption | Models in the database shall be stored encrypted. | Symmetric encryption algorithms such as AES and DES are used. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-013 | Access Control | Before login, the system shall reject all actions that modify website content. | Before successful account login, users cannot upload/modify models or define evolution details. | High | Test |
| NFR-014 | Privilege Control | Non-administrator accounts shall not delete or modify any approved model. | Deletion/modification requests from non-administrators for approved models return rejection results. | High | Test |
| NFR-015 | Spoofing Protection | The system shall detect or block forged login data. | When accessed by an impersonated user, the system detects or blocks forged login data. | High | Test |
| NFR-016 | Tamper Protection | The system shall protect uploaded models and evolution details from arbitrary tampering or deletion. | Unauthorized tampering or deletion operations are rejected and do not affect data integrity. | High | Test |
| NFR-017 | Privacy Compliance | The system shall follow local privacy regulations to protect personal data. | A privacy policy is provided and user consent is obtained. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-018 | License Compliance | Third-party libraries, plugins, and tools shall comply with open-source licenses. | They do not infringe others' intellectual property rights. | Medium | Inspection |
| NFR-019 | Maintainability | When a system fault occurs, it shall be possible to quickly locate and resolve the problem. | Fault location and resolution processes are executable; code is easy to understand, modify, and enhance. | Medium | Inspection |
| NFR-020 | Code Consistency | Code in the same module shall be written in the same format. | Variable names are semantically related, structure and presentation are separated, HTML is used for structure, and CSS is used for layout and presentation. | Medium | Inspection |
| NFR-021 | Portability / Responsive UI | The Web interface shall adapt to different operating systems, browsers, and electronic devices. | It adjusts according to operating system, screen size, screen orientation, and similar environments, and Web content is clearly displayed. | High | Test |
| NFR-022 | Usability | Users shall clearly understand website functions and complete tasks according to prompts. | New users can complete tasks according to prompts; experienced users can complete tasks quickly and efficiently; detailed help documents are provided. | Medium | Demonstration |
| NFR-023 | UI Aesthetics | System pages shall be coordinated, prominent, and easy to understand. | Navigation interfaces do not exceed 10; the main interface is prominent, side and bottom columns are clear, and long pages have bottom navigation. | Medium | Inspection |

## 7. Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
| --- | --- |
| C-001 | The system adopts a B/S architecture, and users access a specific website through a browser. |
| C-002 | The hardware environment shall satisfy x86-64 or ARM CPU, at least 4 GB memory, at least 20 GB available disk, and at least 5 Mbps bandwidth. |
| C-003 | The operating system environment shall support Windows 10 or above 64-bit or Ubuntu 16.04 or above 64-bit, and satisfy cross-platform constraints such as Windows, macOS, and Linux. |
| C-004 | The runtime environment includes mainstream browsers such as Firefox, Chrome, and 360 Browser. |
| C-005 | There are no hardware interfaces. |
| C-006 | Software interfaces include Python libraries and browser Console. |
| C-007 | Communication interfaces include HTTP, HTTPS, TCP, IP, FTP, SMTP, and UDP. |
| C-008 | The databases are N
...[truncated]

samples.gt_source:
@startuml
' Directional setting (Top to Down)
top to bottom direction

' Styling for different layers
skinparam rectangle {
    BackgroundColor<<Frontend>> #e1f5fe
    BorderColor<<Frontend>> #01579b
    
    BackgroundColor<<Presentation>> #f3e5f5
    BorderColor<<Presentation>> #4a148c
    
    BackgroundColor<<Backend>> #fff3e0
    BorderColor<<Backend>> #e65100
}

skinparam database {
    BackgroundColor<<Storage>> #e8f5e9
    BorderColor<<Storage>> #1b5e20
}

' Layers / Subgraphs
package "Frontend Layer" {
    rectangle "React Framework" as React <<Frontend>>
    rectangle "React-Router: Page Navigation" as Router <<Frontend>>
    rectangle "Netron: Node Drawing Features" as NetronFE <<Frontend>>
}

package "Data Presentation Layer" {
    rectangle "RESTful API Design" as RESTAPI <<Presentation>>
    rectangle "Netron: Graph Representation Language" as GraphLang <<Presentation>>
}

package "Backend Layer" {
    rectangle "Flask: Backend Framework" as Flask <<Backend>>
    rectangle "SQLAlchemy: ORM Framework" as SQLAlchemy <<Backend>>
    rectangle "Netron: Model Conversion & Parsing" as NetronBE <<Backend>>
}

package "Storage / Database Layer" {
    database "MySQL: Relational Database" as MySQL <<Storage>>
    database "Neo4j: Graph Database" as Neo4j <<Storage>>
}

' Relationships
React --> Router
React --> NetronFE
React --> RESTAPI
NetronFE --> GraphLang
RESTAPI --> Flask
GraphLang --> Flask
Flask --> SQLAlchemy
Flask --> NetronBE
SQLAlchemy --> MySQL
Flask --> Neo4j

@enduml

completeness.reasoning: The diagram covers the main architectural pillars specified in the requirements, including the B/S architecture, the three logical modules (Presentation, Application, Algorithm), the three actor types, and the two required databases. However, it exhibits significant flaws in its representation. It incorrectly models a protocol (TLS) as a component and creates ambiguity in the application layer by showing redundant data access paths where both a generic 'Backend' component and a specific 'ModelService' connect to the databases, obscuring the actual data flow.

faithfulness.reasoning: The diagram is highly faithful to the requirements. It correctly identifies and includes the specified B/S architecture (C-001), the three user roles as actors, the logical system modules (front end, back end, algorithm support) as packages, and the two required databases (C-008). Inferences, such as adding an 'IdentityProvider', are directly and correctly derived from explicit requirements like 'OAuth 2.0' (C-009).

architectural_rationality.reasoning: The core architectural strategy is sound. Decomposing the backend into services (Auth, Model, User) and using a dedicated algorithm engine are rational choices that align with the functional and non-functional requirements. However, the diagram's depiction of these choices is confusing. The redundant connections from the main 'Backend' component to databases, which are also accessed by specialized services, create a muddled interaction model. Furthermore, modeling TLS as a connectable component is a fundamental representation error.

traceability.reasoning: Traceability is strong. Key architecturally significant requirements (ASRs) can be easily mapped to specific components or relationships. For example, the B/S constraint (C-001) maps to the WebClient/Backend structure, the database constraint (C-008) maps to the MySQL/Neo4jDB components, the OAuth requirement (C-009) maps to the AuthModule and IdentityProvider, and the algorithm dependency (C-006) maps to the AnalysisEngine and PythonLibs.

readability.reasoning: The diagram is only partially readable. The use of packages and clear component naming provides a good high-level overview. However, readability is significantly hindered by the confusing and redundant relationships within the 'Application' package, which forces the reader to guess the true data flow. The incorrect representation of 'TLS' as a component also detracts from clarity and requires the reader to reinterpret the diagrammer's intent.

samples.title: s000040_337afcbb / direct/deepseek-v3.2

project_context.introduction:
### 1.1 Purpose
This SRS defines checked requirements for PlugIt, a framework for portable integration of user-interface-oriented micro-services. The document is prepared as a clean requirements input for downstream architecture diagram generation, implementation review, and verification planning.

### 1.2 Product Scope
PlugIt provides a framework for integrating multiple micro-services through a common proxy-mediated experience while keeping service data and processes separated. The covered scope includes:
- PlugIt Proxy and PlugIt Service topology.
- Service creation and environment-specific configuration.
- PlugIt Proxy API endpoint configuration.
- Programmatic PlugIt API client access.
- Database-backed services with versioned schema upgrades.
- Example mail-driven processing in the standalone proxy workflow.

Mail-driven processing is example/standalone proxy scope and is not a required capability of every core PlugIt service. Specific end-user screen layouts and detailed interaction patterns are not specified.

### 1.3 Intended Audience
- Service developers creating PlugIt-based services.
- Integrators connecting services to a PlugIt Proxy API.
- Operators and administrators configuring databases, private settings, and deployment environments.
- Maintainers of database-backed or mail-integrated PlugIt services.
- Architects generating or reviewing architecture artifacts from requirements.
project_context.asr:
## 5. Non-Functional Requirements

| ID | Quality Attribute | Requirement | Priority | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NFR-001 | Portability | The system shall support deployment of services in multiple environments by externalizing environment-specific settings into configuration files. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-002 | Maintainability | Database schema evolution shall be managed through versioned upgrade files so schema updates can be applied consistently across developer and production environments. | Medium | Inspection |
| NFR-003 | Confidentiality | Private deployment settings shall be kept in local configuration excluded from source control to reduce unintended publication of private settings. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-004 | Reliability | In example/standalone proxy scope, mail processing shall avoid acting on messages with invalid routing hashes or auto-response indicators. | High | Test |

## 7. Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
|---|---|
| C-001 | A database technology must be selected and provisioned externally before database-backed service operation; supported examples include MySQL and SQLite. |
| C-002 | A service that integrates through PlugIt depends on a configured PlugIt Proxy API endpoint URL. |
| C-003 | Network access control configuration is constrained by the `PI_ALLOWED_NETWORKS` CIDR list where that setting is used. |
| C-004 | Mail-driven processing requirements are constrained to the example/standalone proxy workflow unless explicitly promoted to core framework scope. |
| C-005 | Detailed end-user interface layouts and interaction patterns are outside this SRS scope. |

### 2.4 Operating Environment
| Environment | Requirement |
|---|---|
| Proxy environment | The deployment shall provide a reachable PlugIt Proxy API endpoint. |
| Service environment | PlugIt Services shall be configured with service-specific settings and deployment-specific local settings. |
| Database environment | A database technology shall be selected, provisioned, and configured before database-backed service use. Examples include MySQL and SQLite. |
| Internal network topology | PlugIt Proxy and PlugIt Services may be deployed inside an internal network while external access reaches services through the proxy. |
| Standalone proxy mail environment | Example mail processing requires access to inbound email messages and mail routing settings. |

### 3.3 Communication Interfaces
| Interface | Requirement |
|---|---|
| External-to-proxy access | External users or systems shall access PlugIt services through the PlugIt Proxy. |
| Proxy-to-service communication | PlugIt Proxy shall communicate with PlugIt Services through the PlugIt protocol. |
| Service-to-database communication | Each database-backed PlugIt Service shall use its own configured database connection. |
| Email intake | In example/standalone proxy scope, inbound email messages shall be parsed, validated, conditionally processed, and conditionally removed from the mailbox. |

### 2.5 Assumptions and Dependencies
| ID | Assumption / Dependency |
|---|---|
| AD-001 | A PlugIt Proxy API endpoint is available before a service can integrate through the proxy. |
| AD-002 | Database creation, user creation, and connection configuration are handled before database-backed service operation. |
| AD-003 | Private settings are maintained in local configuration and are not published through source control. |
| AD-004 | The protocol and implementation are draft-level and may contain issues or instability. |
| AD-005 | Mail processing behavior applies to the standalone proxy example workflow unless a product design explicitly promotes it to core framework behavior. |

samples.gt_source:
@startuml
skinparam componentStyle rectangle
top to bottom direction

skinparam defaultFontName Arial
skinparam shadowing false
skinparam ArrowColor #333333
skinparam ArrowThickness 1.5
skinparam package {
    BackgroundColor #FFFFFF
    BorderColor #333333
    BorderThickness 1
}
skinparam rectangle {
    BackgroundColor #FFFFFF
    BorderColor #333333
    BorderThickness 1
}
skinparam database {
    BackgroundColor #FFFFFF
    BorderColor #333333
    BorderThickness 1
}
skinparam cloud {
    BackgroundColor #FFFFFF
    BorderColor #333333
    BorderThickness 1
}

cloud "Internet" as Internet

package "Internal Network" as InternalNetwork {
    rectangle "PlugIt Proxy" as PlugItProxy
    database "DB_Proxy" as DbProxy

    rectangle "PlugIt Service 1" as PlugItService1
    rectangle "PlugIt Service 2" as PlugItService2
    rectangle "PlugIt Service 3" as PlugItService3

    database "DB 1" as Db1
    database "DB 2" as Db2
    database "DB 3" as Db3
}

Internet --> PlugItProxy : external access
PlugItProxy ..> PlugItService1 : PlugIt Protocol
PlugItProxy ..> PlugItService2 : PlugIt Protocol
PlugItProxy ..> PlugItService3 : PlugIt Protocol
PlugItProxy --> DbProxy : proxy data access
PlugItService1 --> Db1 : service data
PlugItService2 --> Db2 : service data
PlugItService3 --> Db3 : service data
@enduml
