project_context.introduction:
### 1.1 Purpose
This Software Requirements Specification defines the requirements for the `aws-solutions/iot-device-simulator` system as represented by the provided project materials and reviewed source artifacts. It is intended to support downstream architecture generation and verification planning.

### 1.2 Product Scope
The system is an AWS-based IoT Device Simulator. It provides:
- a web management console,
- simulation and device-type management APIs,
- device simulation execution,
- route data loading for simulation,
- publication of generated simulated-device messages to AWS IoT topics,
- deployment-time custom-resource processing for initialization and supporting resources.

The reviewed materials directly confirm API Gateway configuration, custom-resource response construction, and S3-backed route loading behavior. Broader platform capabilities are included here as product scope context.

### 1.3 Intended Audience
This document is intended for:
- solution architects designing or reviewing the system architecture,
- developers and maintainers implementing or extending the system,
- integrators deploying the solution into AWS environments,
- testers validating API, route-loading, and deployment-related behavior.

### 1.4 References
- Repository: `aws-solutions/iot-device-simulator`
project_context.asr:
## 5. Non-Functional Requirements

### NFR-001 Access Logging
The API deployment shall emit access logs to a log destination using JSON with standard fields.

Verification: Inspection

### NFR-002 Operational Logging Level
The API deployment shall use method logging level `INFO`.

Verification: Inspection

### NFR-003 Tracing
The API deployment shall have tracing enabled.

Verification: Inspection

### NFR-004 API Endpoint Type
The API endpoint type shall be `REGIONAL`.

Verification: Inspection

## 7. System Constraints

### C-001 API Deployment Platform
The REST API is constrained to Amazon API Gateway deployment with stage name `prod`.

### C-002 Allowed HTTP Methods
The API configuration is constrained to methods `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`, and `OPTIONS`.

### C-003 Route Storage Constraint
Route loading is constrained to Amazon S3 object retrieval using the bucket identified by `ROUTE_BUCKET` and a route key resolved from either the provided route name or the generated default route key.

### C-004 Custom-Resource Processing Context
Custom-resource processing is constrained by CloudFormation events, Lambda execution context, and solution ID/version configuration. Stack name shall be supplied as a custom-resource input property.

### 2.4 Operating Environment
The system operates in an AWS-managed environment that includes:
- web console hosting and distribution,
- identity and authentication services,
- Amazon API Gateway,
- AWS Lambda,
- Amazon DynamoDB,
- AWS Step Functions,
- Amazon S3,
- AWS IoT,
- AWS CloudFormation custom resources.

Component-specific runtime context includes:
- API Gateway configured as a regional REST API,
- simulator route loading that depends on an S3 bucket identified by `ROUTE_BUCKET`,
- custom-resource processing that depends on CloudFormation events, Lambda context, and solution versioning configuration.

### 3.3 Communication Interfaces
- The API shall support HTTP methods `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`, and `OPTIONS`.
- The API shall configure CORS preflight behavior for supported methods and headers.
- Allowed CORS request headers shall include `Authorization`, `Content-Type`, `X-Amz-Date`, `X-Amz-Security-Token`, and `X-Api-Key`.

### 2.5 Assumptions and Dependencies
- Route retrieval depends on an S3 bucket identified by `ROUTE_BUCKET`.
- Route loading depends on a route object key derived from either an input `routeName` or a generated default route key.
- API behavior depends on API Gateway request-validation and CORS configuration.
- Custom-resource processing depends on CloudFormation invocation context and solution configuration values.
- Device simulation and message publication depend on AWS service integrations available in the deployed environment.

samples.gt_source:
@startuml
skinparam componentStyle rectangle
left to right direction

skinparam defaultFontName Arial
skinparam shadowing false
skinparam ArrowColor #4A5D78
skinparam ArrowThickness 1.5
skinparam package {
    BackgroundColor #FFFFFF
    BorderColor #555555
    BorderThickness 1
}
skinparam rectangle {
    BackgroundColor #FFFFFF
    BorderColor #555555
    BorderThickness 1
}
skinparam cloud {
    BackgroundColor #FFFFFF
    BorderColor #555555
    BorderThickness 1
}
skinparam database {
    BackgroundColor #FFFFFF
    BorderColor #555555
    BorderThickness 1
}

package "AWS IoT Device Simulator" as IotDeviceSimulator {
    rectangle "Users" as Users
    rectangle "Browser Web UI\nstatic app client" as BrowserWebUi
    cloud "Amazon CloudFront" as CloudFront
    database "Amazon S3\nWeb UI bucket" as WebUiBucket
    rectangle "Amazon Cognito" as Cognito
    rectangle "Amazon API Gateway\nREST API" as ApiGateway
    rectangle "AWS Lambda\nSimulator API" as LambdaSimulator
    database "Amazon DynamoDB\nSimulator state" as DynamoDb
    database "Amazon S3\nRoute data bucket" as RouteBucket
    rectangle "AWS Step Functions\nSimulation workflow" as StepFunctions
    cloud "AWS IoT Core" as IotCore
    rectangle "AWS CloudFormation\nCustom resources" as CloudFormation
}

Users --> CloudFront : web app request
CloudFront --> WebUiBucket : fetch web assets
CloudFront --> BrowserWebUi : deliver static web UI
BrowserWebUi --> Cognito : authenticate
Cognito --> BrowserWebUi : auth tokens
BrowserWebUi --> ApiGateway : API requests
ApiGateway --> LambdaSimulator : REST integration
LambdaSimulator <--> DynamoDb : simulator metadata and state
LambdaSimulator --> RouteBucket : get route JSON\nby routeName
RouteBucket --> LambdaSimulator : route JSON
LambdaSimulator --> StepFunctions : start simulation workflow
StepFunctions --> IotCore : publish simulated device messages
CloudFormation --> WebUiBucket : deploy/configure\nsolution assets
CloudFormation --> LambdaSimulator : custom-resource setup
@enduml

project_context.introduction:
### 1.1 Purpose
This SRS defines the final checked requirements for Foot365, an AWS-based football information web application. The document is prepared as a clean requirements input for downstream architecture diagram generation.

### 1.2 Product Scope
Foot365 provides football match information, live score updates, machine-learning match predictions, nearby match-screening recommendations, conversational football queries, and request-based SMS reminders.

The term "live streaming" in the product context shall mean real-time live-score updates through the streaming data pipeline and live match-screening recommendations. The system shall not be treated as providing in-application video streaming unless a separate video-streaming requirement is added.

The in-scope capabilities are:
- Viewing fixtures, standings, schedules, results, and live scores.
- Viewing match prediction probabilities.
- Finding nearby places that screen live football matches.
- Asking football information questions through a chatbot interface.
- Viewing or requesting fixture reminders.
- Requesting SMS reminder notifications.
- Exchanging frontend/backend data through AWS API Gateway.
- Persisting and searching match results, statistics, and fixture data.
- Processing live score updates through Kafka and Apache Avro on EC2.
- Deploying the frontend, backend, data, notification, prediction, and streaming components on AWS services.

### 1.3 Intended Audience
- Product owners defining Foot365 behavior.
- Developers implementing frontend, backend, chatbot, prediction, notification, data, and deployment components.
- Testers validating user-facing football workflows and AWS integrations.
- Architecture reviewers generating or validating architecture diagrams.
- Deployers configuring the AWS runtime environment.

### 1.4 Terminology
| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| Fixture | A scheduled football match. |
| Standing | A team's ranking or table position within a competition. |
| Live score | A real-time score update for a match. |
| Screening recommendation | A nearby venue recommendation where users can watch a live match. |
| Chatbot | A conversational interface that accepts text messages and returns football information responses. |
| Prediction | A machine-learning output indicating likely match outcomes such as home win, draw, or away win. |
| Reminder | A user-requested notification about an upcoming game. |
project_context.asr:
## 5. Non-Functional Requirements

| ID | Requirement | Quality Attribute | Priority | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NFR-001 | Protected application access shall use Cognito and IAM for authentication and security management. | Security | High | Inspection |
| NFR-002 | API interactions shall use `application/json` as the default content type and accept type unless explicitly overridden. | Interoperability | High | Test |
| NFR-003 | The system shall be deployable using AWS-managed or AWS-hosted components including S3, API Gateway, Lambda, DynamoDB, SNS, SQS, SageMaker, EC2, Cognito, IAM, and Elasticsearch. | Deployability | High | Inspection |
| NFR-004 | The system shall rely on DynamoDB and Elasticsearch to support growth in stored and requested match data, without defining a quantitative throughput, latency, or capacity target in this SRS. | Scalability | Medium | Architecture analysis |
| NFR-005 | The live score pipeline shall use a streaming data mechanism suitable for real-time score update processing. | Timeliness | Medium | Analysis |

## 7. System Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
|---|---|
| C-001 | Frontend implementation shall use HTML, CSS, Bootstrap, and jQuery. |
| C-002 | Backend implementation shall use Python and Node. |
| C-003 | Static frontend hosting and relevant file storage shall use Amazon S3. |
| C-004 | Backend APIs shall be exposed through API Gateway and Lambda. |
| C-005 | Authentication and security management shall use Cognito and IAM. |
| C-006 | Notifications shall use SQS and SNS for queueing and SMS delivery. |
| C-007 | Match predictions shall use SageMaker. |
| C-008 | Match data storage and retrieval shall use DynamoDB and Elasticsearch. |
| C-009 | Live score update processing shall use Kafka with Apache Avro running on EC2. |
| C-010 | API integration shall use the `execute-api` service configuration and JSON content defaults. |
| C-011 | Service interaction details beyond the explicitly stated requirements shall be finalized in the architecture model rather than assumed by this SRS. |

### 2.4 Operating Environment
- Web frontend hosted on Amazon S3.
- Backend services exposed through Amazon API Gateway and AWS Lambda.
- Authentication and security management through Amazon Cognito and IAM.
- Match data storage in DynamoDB and match data search through Elasticsearch.
- Prediction service using Amazon SageMaker.
- Notification workflow using SQS and SNS.
- Live score update pipeline on EC2 using Kafka with Apache Avro.
- API calls using AWS Signature Version 4 and the `execute-api` service configuration.

### 3.3 Communication Interfaces
| ID | Interface | Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| COM-001 | HTTP API requests | API requests shall support HTTP verb, path, query parameters, headers, and body content. |
| COM-002 | Signed AWS API calls | API requests to backend services shall support AWS Signature Version 4 with `execute-api` as the service name. |
| COM-003 | Streaming score updates | Live score events shall be processed through Kafka messages encoded with Apache Avro. |
| COM-004 | Notification queueing | Reminder requests shall be queued before SMS notification delivery. |

### 2.5 Assumptions and Dependencies
- The system depends on the configured AWS service stack for hosting, routing, authentication, prediction, notification, storage, search, and streaming.
- API requests and responses use `application/json` by default unless explicitly overridden.
- Final-week season predictions assume that all matches played in the current season except the last week are used as input data.
- Match-screening recommendations depend on available location or city information supplied by the user.
- SMS reminder delivery depends on notification service configuration.

skinparam componentStyle rectangle
left to right direction

skinparam defaultFontName Arial
skinparam shadowing false

    BorderColor #111111
    BorderThickness 1
}

    BackgroundColor #FFFFFF
    BorderColor #111111
    BorderThickness 1
}

    BackgroundColor #FFFFFF
    BorderColor #111111
    BorderThickness 1
}

    BackgroundColor #FFFFFF
    BorderColor #111111
    BorderThickness 1
}

    BackgroundColor #FFFFFF
    BorderColor #111111
    BorderThickness 1
}

rectangle "User" as User
cloud "S3" as S3
rectangle "Cognito" as Cognito
rectangle "API Gateway" as ApiGateway

package "Live Score Pipeline" as LiveScorePipeline {
    rectangle "live_score\nLambda" as LiveScoreLambda
    database "DynamoDB\nLive Scores" as LiveScoreDynamo
    database "Elasticsearch" as LiveScoreSearch
    node "EC2" as LiveScoreEC2 {
        queue "Kafka" as Kafka
        rectangle "M4" as M4
        rectangle "foot app" as FootApp
    }
}

package "Standing Service" as StandingService {
    rectangle "standing\nLambda" as StandingLambda
    database "DynamoDB\nStandings" as StandingDynamo
    database "Elasticsearch" as StandingSearch
}

package "Recommendation Service" as RecommendationService {
    rectangle "recommendation\nLambda" as RecommendationLambda
    rectangle "Lex" as Lex
}

package "Fixtures Service" as FixturesService {
    rectangle "fixtures\nLambda" as FixturesLambda
    queue "SQS" as Sqs
}

package "Notification Service" as NotificationService {
    queue "SQS" as NotificationSqs
    rectangle "SNS" as Sns
}

package "Prediction Service" as PredictionService {
    rectangle "prediction\nLambda" as PredictionLambda
    rectangle "SageMaker" as SageMaker
    database "DynamoDB\nPredictions" as PredictionDynamo
    database "Elasticsearch" as PredictionSearch
}

User --> S3 : load web app
User --> Cognito : sign in
User --> ApiGateway : API request
ApiGateway ..> Cognito : authorize token

ApiGateway --> LiveScoreLambda : live_score
ApiGateway --> StandingLambda : standing
ApiGateway --> RecommendationLambda : recommendation
ApiGateway --> FixturesLambda : fixtures
ApiGateway --> PredictionLambda : prediction

LiveScoreLambda --> LiveScoreDynamo
LiveScoreLambda --> LiveScoreSearch
FootApp --> M4
M4 --> Kafka
Kafka --> LiveScoreLambda : stream events

StandingLambda --> StandingDynamo
StandingLambda --> StandingSearch

RecommendationLambda --> Lex
RecommendationLambda --> LiveScoreSearch
RecommendationLambda --> StandingSearch

FixturesLambda --> Sqs
Sqs --> NotificationSqs
NotificationSqs --> Sns
Sns --> User : SMS reminder

PredictionLambda --> SageMaker
PredictionLambda --> PredictionDynamo
PredictionLambda --> PredictionSearch
SageMaker --> PredictionDynamo
@enduml

project_context.introduction:
### 1.1 Purpose
This SRS defines checked requirements for PlugIt, a framework for portable integration of user-interface-oriented micro-services. The document is prepared as a clean requirements input for downstream architecture diagram generation, implementation review, and verification planning.

### 1.2 Product Scope
PlugIt provides a framework for integrating multiple micro-services through a common proxy-mediated experience while keeping service data and processes separated. The covered scope includes:
- PlugIt Proxy and PlugIt Service topology.
- Service creation and environment-specific configuration.
- PlugIt Proxy API endpoint configuration.
- Programmatic PlugIt API client access.
- Database-backed services with versioned schema upgrades.
- Example mail-driven processing in the standalone proxy workflow.

Mail-driven processing is example/standalone proxy scope and is not a required capability of every core PlugIt service. Specific end-user screen layouts and detailed interaction patterns are not specified.

### 1.3 Intended Audience
- Service developers creating PlugIt-based services.
- Integrators connecting services to a PlugIt Proxy API.
- Operators and administrators configuring databases, private settings, and deployment environments.
- Maintainers of database-backed or mail-integrated PlugIt services.
- Architects generating or reviewing architecture artifacts from requirements.
project_context.asr:
## 5. Non-Functional Requirements

| ID | Quality Attribute | Requirement | Priority | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NFR-001 | Portability | The system shall support deployment of services in multiple environments by externalizing environment-specific settings into configuration files. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-002 | Maintainability | Database schema evolution shall be managed through versioned upgrade files so schema updates can be applied consistently across developer and production environments. | Medium | Inspection |
| NFR-003 | Confidentiality | Private deployment settings shall be kept in local configuration excluded from source control to reduce unintended publication of private settings. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-004 | Reliability | In example/standalone proxy scope, mail processing shall avoid acting on messages with invalid routing hashes or auto-response indicators. | High | Test |

## 7. Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
|---|---|
| C-001 | A database technology must be selected and provisioned externally before database-backed service operation; supported examples include MySQL and SQLite. |
| C-002 | A service that integrates through PlugIt depends on a configured PlugIt Proxy API endpoint URL. |
| C-003 | Network access control configuration is constrained by the `PI_ALLOWED_NETWORKS` CIDR list where that setting is used. |
| C-004 | Mail-driven processing requirements are constrained to the example/standalone proxy workflow unless explicitly promoted to core framework scope. |
| C-005 | Detailed end-user interface layouts and interaction patterns are outside this SRS scope. |

### 2.4 Operating Environment
| Environment | Requirement |
|---|---|
| Proxy environment | The deployment shall provide a reachable PlugIt Proxy API endpoint. |
| Service environment | PlugIt Services shall be configured with service-specific settings and deployment-specific local settings. |
| Database environment | A database technology shall be selected, provisioned, and configured before database-backed service use. Examples include MySQL and SQLite. |
| Internal network topology | PlugIt Proxy and PlugIt Services may be deployed inside an internal network while external access reaches services through the proxy. |
| Standalone proxy mail environment | Example mail processing requires access to inbound email messages and mail routing settings. |

### 3.3 Communication Interfaces
| Interface | Requirement |
|---|---|
| External-to-proxy access | External users or systems shall access PlugIt services through the PlugIt Proxy. |
| Proxy-to-service communication | PlugIt Proxy shall communicate with PlugIt Services through the PlugIt protocol. |
| Service-to-database communication | Each database-backed PlugIt Service shall use its own configured database connection. |
| Email intake | In example/standalone proxy scope, inbound email messages shall be parsed, validated, conditionally processed, and conditionally removed from the mailbox. |

### 2.5 Assumptions and Dependencies
| ID | Assumption / Dependency |
|---|---|
| AD-001 | A PlugIt Proxy API endpoint is available before a service can integrate through the proxy. |
| AD-002 | Database creation, user creation, and connection configuration are handled before database-backed service operation. |
| AD-003 | Private settings are maintained in local configuration and are not published through source control. |
| AD-004 | The protocol and implementation are draft-level and may contain issues or instability. |
| AD-005 | Mail processing behavior applies to the standalone proxy example workflow unless a product design explicitly promotes it to core framework behavior. |

PlugItProxy ..> PlugItService3 : PlugIt Protocol
PlugItProxy --> DbProxy : proxy data access
PlugItService1 --> Db1 : service data
PlugItService2 --> Db2 : service data
PlugItService3 --> Db3 : service data
@enduml

project_context.introduction:
### 1.1 Purpose
This SRS defines the final checked requirements for a containerized song mood storage, retrieval, and search web application. It is prepared as a clean requirements input for downstream architecture diagram generation.

### 1.2 Product Scope
The system is a multi-tier web application that allows users to request song mood information. It provides:
- A browser-accessible React frontend.
- A FastAPI backend for song lookup and storage workflows.
- Elasticsearch storage for song records and associated mood values.
- Kibana as an auxiliary UI for inspecting stored Elasticsearch data.
- Genius API integration for searching songs that are not already stored.
- Dockerized deployment with separate container services for frontend, backend, Elasticsearch, and Kibana.

The system stores and retrieves song-mood associations for songs whose mood is already available. A runtime mood classification or model-inference component is not specified as an in-scope requirement unless added separately.

The project dataset scope is English songs. This is a dataset and project-scope constraint, not a runtime language-rejection requirement.

### 1.3 Intended Audience
- End users requesting song mood information through the web application.
- Developers maintaining the React frontend and FastAPI backend.
- Operators deploying and updating the containerized services.
- Testers validating lookup, storage, and external API behavior.
- Architecture reviewers generating or validating architecture diagrams.

### 1.4 Terminology
| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| Song record | A stored song data item containing song identity and lyric metadata. |
| Mood association | A stored relationship between a song and an already available mood value. |
| Known song | A song that already has a stored record in Elasticsearch. |
| Missing song | A requested song that is not found in Elasticsearch and must be searched through the Genius API. |
| Auxiliary data UI | Kibana, used for inspecting data stored in Elasticsearch. |
project_context.asr:
## 5. Non-Functional Requirements

| ID | Requirement | Quality Attribute | Priority | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NFR-001 | The system shall be deployable as a multi-tier Dockerized application with separate container services for frontend, backend, Elasticsearch, and Kibana. | Deployability / portability | High | Inspection |
| NFR-002 | Stored song-mood associations shall be retrievable from Elasticsearch for later requests. | Retrieval efficiency | Medium | Test |
| NFR-003 | The frontend shall be accessible through a web browser during development and operation. | Browser compatibility | Medium | Demonstration |
| NFR-004 | The backend shall use Elasticsearch as the authoritative store for persisted song-mood associations. | Data consistency | High | Inspection |

## 7. System Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
|---|---|
| C-001 | Frontend technology shall be React. |
| C-002 | Backend technology shall be FastAPI. |
| C-003 | Persistent song and mood storage shall use Elasticsearch. |
| C-004 | Kibana shall be used as the auxiliary UI for Elasticsearch data inspection. |
| C-005 | The backend shall depend on the Genius API for songs not found in Elasticsearch. |
| C-006 | The deployment shall include separate Docker container services for frontend, backend, Elasticsearch, and Kibana. |
| C-007 | The Genius API shall be modeled as an external dependency rather than an internal container service. |
| C-008 | The project dataset scope shall be English songs. |
| C-009 | A runtime classifier, model server, or inference algorithm shall not be assumed unless specified by a separate requirement. |

### 2.4 Operating Environment
- Web browser for frontend access.
- Docker-based runtime for the application stack.
- React frontend container.
- FastAPI backend container.
- Elasticsearch container.
- Kibana container.
- External network access from the backend to the Genius API.

### 3.3 Communication Interfaces
| ID | Interface | Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| COM-001 | Browser access | Users shall access the frontend through standard browser web access. |
| COM-002 | Internal service communication | Frontend, backend, Elasticsearch, and Kibana shall communicate across the Dockerized application network according to their configured endpoints. |
| COM-003 | External API communication | The backend shall communicate with the Genius API as an external service. |

### 2.5 Assumptions and Dependencies
- Elasticsearch is required for stored song and mood retrieval.
- The Genius API is required for searching songs that are not stored.
- Kibana depends on Elasticsearch availability.
- The backend depends on Elasticsearch availability for known-song lookup.
- The backend depends on Genius API availability for missing-song search.
- English-only scope applies to the project dataset used by the application and analysis.

project_context.introduction:
### Purpose
This SRS defines the checked software requirements for Turing and its related runtime components. It is limited to repository-supported behavior and interfaces reflected in the reviewed source materials.

### Product scope
Turing provides:
- an HTTP API for Turing resources, including routers and ensemblers,
- cluster-facing components for creating, updating, and deleting Turing router deployments in Kubernetes,
- a PyFuncEnsembler webservice runtime for user-defined ensemblers.

### Intended audience
This document is intended for:
- product owners and maintainers of Turing,
- API consumers integrating with Turing endpoints,
- QA and verification engineers,
- deployment and platform engineers operating Turing in Kubernetes-based environments.
project_context.asr:
## 5. Non-Functional Requirements

| ID | Quality attribute | Requirement | Priority | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NFR-001 | Security and validation | The API service shall provide an HTTP middleware layer for authorization checks and OpenAPI-spec-based request validation; authorization middleware shall be mounted when authorization configuration is enabled, and request validation middleware shall be mounted when OpenAPI validation configuration is enabled. | High | Inspection and configuration test |
| NFR-002 | Observability | The Router component shall integrate Jaeger tracing capability; the system shall initialize a Jaeger client to support request tracing, but whether a specific request receives a trace shall be determined by the tracer enabled state or sampling configuration. | Medium | Demonstration |
| NFR-003 | Configurability | The Router user container shall use port `8080` by default and shall allow this port to be overridden through `ports.containerPort` in deployment configuration. | Medium | Test |
| NFR-004 | Interoperability | API specifications shall conform to OpenAPI `3.0.3` for documented endpoints and schemas. | Medium | Inspection |

## 7. System Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
|---|---|
| C-001 | The API is constrained to OpenAPI-defined endpoints and schemas, including OpenAPI version `3.0.3`. |
| C-002 | Router deployment lifecycle management is constrained to a Kubernetes cluster environment. |
| C-003 | Local Docker image building for the PyFuncEnsembler service requires prior download of model artifacts from an MLflow model registry. |
| C-004 | Router user containers use port `8080` by default unless overridden through `ports.containerPort`. |

### Operating environment
- HTTP-based API server
- Kubernetes cluster for router deployment lifecycle management
- Docker-based packaging for the PyFuncEnsembler service
- MLflow model registry as an artifact source for local image building of the PyFuncEnsembler service

### Software/API interfaces
| Interface | Requirement summary |
|---|---|
| OpenAPI-defined API | API handlers and routing shall be defined by OpenAPI specifications. |
| Kubernetes cluster interface | The system shall use Kubernetes interfaces for creating, updating, and deleting Turing router deployments. |
| MLflow model registry | The PyFuncEnsembler local image build flow shall use model artifacts downloaded from an MLflow model registry. |
| Middleware layer | The API service shall provide an HTTP middleware layer for authorization checks and OpenAPI-spec-based request validation, subject to configuration. |

### Communication interfaces
| Interface | Requirement summary |
|---|---|
| HTTP | API endpoints shall be exposed over HTTP. |
| JSON payloads | Ensembler API responses shall use `application/json` where documented. |

### Assumptions and dependencies
- API behavior is defined through OpenAPI specifications.
- Router deployment operations depend on Kubernetes cluster access.
- PyFuncEnsembler image creation depends on downloading model artifacts from an MLflow model registry.
- Request tracing may integrate with Jaeger.
- Middleware behavior for authorization and OpenAPI request validation depends on service configuration.

rectangle "Turing User" as TuringUser
rectangle "Router Clients" as RouterClients
rectangle "Router Endpoints" as RouterEndpoints

package "Turing System" as TuringSystem {
    rectangle "Turing UI\nWeb interface to configure Routers" as TuringUi
    rectangle "Turing API Server\nHTTP REST API to configure Routers" as TuringApiServer
    database "Database\nPersistent storage for Router objects" as RouterDatabase
}

rectangle "Secret Store" as SecretStore
rectangle "Authorization Service" as AuthorizationService

package "Kubernetes Cluster" as KubernetesCluster {
    rectangle "Knative Controller" as KnativeController
    rectangle "Istio" as Istio
}

rectangle "Experimentation Service" as ExperimentationService
rectangle "Machine Learning Platform\nMLP API Server" as MlpApiServer
rectangle "Monitoring and Alerting Service" as MonitoringService

TuringUser --> TuringUi : use
TuringUi --> TuringApiServer : make HTTP calls
TuringApiServer --> RouterDatabase : save and retrieve routers

RouterClients --> RouterEndpoints : requests from router clients
TuringApiServer --> RouterEndpoints : create endpoints\nfor accessing routers

TuringApiServer --> SecretStore : save and retrieve\ncredentials
TuringApiServer --> AuthorizationService : check user\npermissions
TuringApiServer --> KubernetesCluster : deploy router\nworkloads
TuringApiServer --> ExperimentationService : retrieve experiments\nconfigured for routers
TuringApiServer --> MlpApiServer : retrieve projects and\nenvironments for routers
TuringApiServer --> MonitoringService : configure alerts based\non router metrics

KubernetesCluster --> KnativeController : manage router services
KubernetesCluster --> Istio : expose router traffic
Istio --> RouterEndpoints : route client traffic
MonitoringService --> KubernetesCluster : retrieve router metrics
@enduml

project_context.introduction:
### Purpose
This SRS defines checked software requirements for the `teamgram/teamgram-server` product. It consolidates repository-backed requirements into a normalized specification suitable for downstream architecture and design activities.

### Product scope
The product is an open-source server system for Telegram-compatible clients using MTProto-related protocols and services. The repository also documents deployment and operation using Docker or manual build/install, integration with infrastructure services, protocol handling for authorization keys, salts, sessions, and selected MTProto service messages.

For protocol behaviors involving authorization keys, server salt, encrypted sessions, future salts, and related session interactions, this SRS relies conservatively on protocol documentation together with repository implementation cues. Requirements are therefore framed as server support for protocol-required interactions rather than as stronger claims about full protocol-flow guarantees unless directly supported.

### Intended audience
This document is intended for:
- Product maintainers and backend engineers
- Architects and integration engineers
- Test engineers
- Operators deploying the server and its required infrastructure
project_context.asr:
## 5. Non-Functional Requirements

| ID | Requirement | Priority | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|
| NFR-001 | The system shall be implemented in Go. | High | Inspection |
| NFR-002 | The system shall maintain interoperability with Telegram-compatible clients through its MTProto server behavior. | High | Demonstration |
| NFR-003 | The system shall support deployment through documented Docker-based and manual build/install paths. | Medium | Demonstration |
| NFR-004 | Where the repository’s media wrapper capability is used, the runtime environment shall provide FFmpeg and FFProbe. | Low | Inspection |

## 7. System Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
|---|---|
| C-001 | The product is constrained to a Go implementation. |
| C-002 | Deployment is constrained by dependencies on MySQL, Redis, etcd, Kafka, and MinIO. |
| C-003 | The documented deployment paths are Docker-based startup and manual build/install. |
| C-004 | The fixed verification code `12345` applies only to the default or none-verifier/test configuration; deployments using an external verification service are constrained by that service’s returned and validated code. |
| C-005 | Use of the media wrapper capability is constrained by the availability of FFmpeg and FFProbe. |

### Operating environment
The system operates in an environment that includes:
- A Go runtime/build environment
- Docker-based deployment or startup support
- External infrastructure services: MySQL, Redis, etcd, Kafka, and MinIO
- Optional FFmpeg and FFProbe tools where the media wrapper component is used

### Software/API interfaces
The system shall provide or use the following software interfaces:
- An MTProto-compatible server interface for Telegram-compatible clients
- Service-message handling interfaces for messages including `rpc_drop_answer` and `destroy_session`
- Integration interfaces to MySQL, Redis, etcd, Kafka, and MinIO
- An optional FFmpeg/FFProbe wrapper interface where media-processing functionality is used

### Communication interfaces
The system shall support:
- MTProto client-server communication
- Encrypted session communication after authorization-related setup
- RPC and service-message exchanges needed for supported MTProto interactions

### Assumptions and dependencies
- Clients are compatible with Telegram/MTProto expectations used by the server
- Deployment includes the required infrastructure services
- Protocol-facing session, salt, and service-message behavior follows documented MTProto semantics
- If the optional media wrapper capability is used, FFmpeg and FFProbe are available in the runtime environment

    rectangle "Web" as WebClient
    rectangle "PC" as PcClient
}

package "Access Layer" as AccessLayer {
    rectangle "Gateway" as Gateway
    rectangle "Auth Session" as AuthSession
    rectangle "Session" as Session
    rectangle "Status" as Status
}

rectangle "BFF" as Bff

package "Services" as ServiceLayer {
    rectangle "DFS" as Dfs
    rectangle "Services" as Services
    rectangle "Msg" as Msg
}

package "Storages" as Storages {
    database "Redis" as Redis
    database "MySQL" as Mysql
    database "MinIO" as Minio
}

rectangle "Push" as Push
rectangle "Sync" as Sync
queue "Kafka" as Kafka

IosClient <--> Gateway : tcp
AndroidClient <--> Gateway : tcp
WebClient <--> Gateway : websocket
PcClient <--> Gateway : websocket

Gateway <--> Session : session routing
Session --> AuthSession : auth state
Session --> Status : presence status
Session --> Redis : session state
Session --> Bff : rpc

Bff --> Dfs : file rpc
Bff --> Services : business rpc
Bff --> Msg : message rpc
Bff --> Kafka : publish async events
Sync --> Gateway : rpc

Services --> Redis : cache data
Services --> Mysql : persistent data
Dfs --> Minio : object storage
Msg --> Kafka : publish message events
Kafka --> Sync : message stream
Sync --> Push : push delivery
@enduml

project_context.introduction:
### 1.1 Purpose
This SRS defines the final checked requirements for a containerized song mood storage, retrieval, and search web application. It is prepared as a clean requirements input for downstream architecture diagram generation.

### 1.2 Product Scope
The system is a multi-tier web application that allows users to request song mood information. It provides:
- A browser-accessible React frontend.
- A FastAPI backend for song lookup and storage workflows.
- Elasticsearch storage for song records and associated mood values.
- Kibana as an auxiliary UI for inspecting stored Elasticsearch data.
- Genius API integration for searching songs that are not already stored.
- Dockerized deployment with separate container services for frontend, backend, Elasticsearch, and Kibana.

The system stores and retrieves song-mood associations for songs whose mood is already available. A runtime mood classification or model-inference component is not specified as an in-scope requirement unless added separately.

The project dataset scope is English songs. This is a dataset and project-scope constraint, not a runtime language-rejection requirement.

### 1.3 Intended Audience
- End users requesting song mood information through the web application.
- Developers maintaining the React frontend and FastAPI backend.
- Operators deploying and updating the containerized services.
- Testers validating lookup, storage, and external API behavior.
- Architecture reviewers generating or validating architecture diagrams.

### 1.4 Terminology
| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| Song record | A stored song data item containing song identity and lyric metadata. |
| Mood association | A stored relationship between a song and an already available mood value. |
| Known song | A song that already has a stored record in Elasticsearch. |
| Missing song | A requested song that is not found in Elasticsearch and must be searched through the Genius API. |
| Auxiliary data UI | Kibana, used for inspecting data stored in Elasticsearch. |
project_context.asr:
## 5. Non-Functional Requirements

| ID | Requirement | Quality Attribute | Priority | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NFR-001 | The system shall be deployable as a multi-tier Dockerized application with separate container services for frontend, backend, Elasticsearch, and Kibana. | Deployability / portability | High | Inspection |
| NFR-002 | Stored song-mood associations shall be retrievable from Elasticsearch for later requests. | Retrieval efficiency | Medium | Test |
| NFR-003 | The frontend shall be accessible through a web browser during development and operation. | Browser compatibility | Medium | Demonstration |
| NFR-004 | The backend shall use Elasticsearch as the authoritative store for persisted song-mood associations. | Data consistency | High | Inspection |

## 7. System Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
|---|---|
| C-001 | Frontend technology shall be React. |
| C-002 | Backend technology shall be FastAPI. |
| C-003 | Persistent song and mood storage shall use Elasticsearch. |
| C-004 | Kibana shall be used as the auxiliary UI for Elasticsearch data inspection. |
| C-005 | The backend shall depend on the Genius API for songs not found in Elasticsearch. |
| C-006 | The deployment shall include separate Docker container services for frontend, backend, Elasticsearch, and Kibana. |
| C-007 | The Genius API shall be modeled as an external dependency rather than an internal container service. |
| C-008 | The project dataset scope shall be English songs. |
| C-009 | A runtime classifier, model server, or inference algorithm shall not be assumed unless specified by a separate requirement. |

### 2.4 Operating Environment
- Web browser for frontend access.
- Docker-based runtime for the application stack.
- React frontend container.
- FastAPI backend container.
- Elasticsearch container.
- Kibana container.
- External network access from the backend to the Genius API.

### 3.3 Communication Interfaces
| ID | Interface | Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| COM-001 | Browser access | Users shall access the frontend through standard browser web access. |
| COM-002 | Internal service communication | Frontend, backend, Elasticsearch, and Kibana shall communicate across the Dockerized application network according to their configured endpoints. |
| COM-003 | External API communication | The backend shall communicate with the Genius API as an external service. |

### 2.5 Assumptions and Dependencies
- Elasticsearch is required for stored song and mood retrieval.
- The Genius API is required for searching songs that are not stored.
- Kibana depends on Elasticsearch availability.
- The backend depends on Elasticsearch availability for known-song lookup.
- The backend depends on Genius API availability for missing-song search.
- English-only scope applies to the project dataset used by the application and analysis.

samples.title: s000034_43fffc9f / direct/gpt-5

project_context.introduction:
### 1.1 Purpose
This Software Requirements Specification defines normalized requirements for `torc_py`, a Python library for task-based parallelism. The library enables Python applications to express function evaluations as tasks, execute those tasks across worker threads and MPI processes, collect results, use callbacks, switch between task-parallel and SPMD execution, and apply adaptive load balancing through task stealing.

### 1.2 Scope
The system scope includes:
- a Python package imported as `torc`,
- task submission, waiting, result retrieval, and completion iteration,
- parallel map behavior,
- callback tasks associated with completed parent tasks,
- cyclic worker assignment when no target worker is specified,
- directed submission to a worker queue when a target queue identifier is specified,
- optional task stealing between MPI processes,
- use on shared-memory and distributed-memory platforms,
- internal MPI and multithreading support,
- compatibility with application-level MPI routines,
- configuration through environment variables,
- documented examples for master-worker execution, SPMD switching, numerical optimization, image preprocessing, and task stealing.

This SRS specifies requirements and runtime concepts. Architecture generation should derive structure from this document rather than rely on a pre-specified topology.

### 1.3 Intended Audience
- Python developers parallelizing function evaluations.
- HPC users running workloads across worker threads, MPI processes, or cluster nodes.
- Testers validating task execution, result retrieval, map behavior, callback behavior, and task stealing.
- Maintainers extending the Python tasking package and example suite.
- Architecture-generation tools consuming a clean SRS without review metadata.

### 1.4 Definitions and Abbreviations
| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| Task | A submitted asynchronous function evaluation. |
| Future | Task descriptor that provides access to task input and result values. |
| Primary application task | The main application task launched on MPI rank 0. |
| Worker thread | Thread that executes task functions. |
| MPI process | Process participating in MPI-based execution. |
| Rank | MPI process identifier. |
| Queue identifier (`qid`) | Target worker identifier for task submission; `-1` means cyclic assignment. |
| SPMD | Single Program Multiple Data execution mode. |
| Task stealing | Mechanism allowing idle workers to retrieve tasks from another queue. |
| HDF5 | File format used by the image preprocessing example output. |
project_context.asr:
## 5. Non-Functional Requirements

| ID | Requirement | Quality attribute | Priority | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NFR-001 | The system shall provide one Python-facing tasking model intended for both shared-memory and distributed-memory execution. Acceptance is based on product description and examples; no automated cross-platform acceptance test is specified here. | Portability | Medium | Inspection |
| NFR-002 | The system shall maintain compatibility with application-level MPI routines while hiding internal MPI communication details from normal task submission users. | Compatibility | High | Inspection |
| NFR-003 | The system shall support parallel execution patterns where four worker threads, configured as two MPI processes with two workers each, can execute four one-second tasks in approximately one task-duration. | Performance | Medium | Demonstration |
| NFR-004 | The system shall support adaptive load balancing through task stealing when idle workers detect empty local queues and another queue has pending tasks. | Load balancing | Medium | Demonstration |
| NFR-005 | The system shall support cluster-scale scheduling use cases; the reported TMCMC/Pi4U result of more than 90% parallel efficiency on 1024 compute nodes shall be treated as a documented use-case result, not a guaranteed minimum for every workload. | Scalability | Medium | Analysis |
| NFR-006 | The system shall support nested parallelism and map-style parallel loops for Python applications. | Modifiability | Medium | Demonstration |

## 7. Constraints

| ID | Constraint | Type |
|---|---|---|
| C-001 | The product shall be consumed through the Python module interface `import torc`. | Interface |
| C-002 | The product shall depend on MPI and `mpi4py` for MPI-based operation. | Technology |
| C-003 | The product shall use Python multithreading for worker execution. | Technology |
| C-004 | `TORC_WORKERS` shall configure the number of worker threads used by each MPI process; default value is `1`. | Runtime configuration |
| C-005 | `TORC_STEALING` shall configure whether inter-process task stealing is enabled; default value is `False`. | Runtime configuration |
| C-006 | `TORC_SERVER_YIELDTIME` and `TORC_WORKER_YIELDTIME` shall configure idle-yield timing for server and worker threads. | Runtime configuration |
| C-007 | Some callback examples require one worker thread per MPI process. | Example constraint |
| C-008 | Optional examples may require `numpy`, `cma`, `h5py`, or `pillow`. | Dependency |
| C-009 | Test and example execution may require `mpirun` and the ability to pass environment variables to MPI processes. | Execution environment |
| C-010 | Test materials include Eclipse Public License v1.0 notices. | License |

### 2.4 Operating Environment
| Environment aspect | Requirement context |
|---|---|
| Python runtime | The library is imported and used from Python applications. |
| MPI runtime | Multi-process execution uses MPI and `mpi4py`. |
| Worker threads | Each MPI process may use one or more worker threads. |
| Shared-memory platform | The same tasking model is intended to run on shared-memory systems. |
| Distributed-memory platform | The same tasking model is intended to run across MPI processes and cluster nodes. |
| Environment variables | Runtime behavior is configured through `TORC_WORKERS`, `TORC_STEALING`, `TORC_SERVER_YIELDTIME`, and `TORC_WORKER_YIELDTIME`. |
| Optional packages | Some examples require packages such as `numpy`, `cma`, `h5py`, and `pillow`. |

### 3.3 Communication Interfaces
| ID | Interface | Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| CI-001 | Internal MPI communication | The system shall use MPI internally for communication across MPI processes. |
| CI-002 | Worker-thread scheduling | The system shall schedule submitted tasks on worker threads. |
| CI-003 | Task stealing requests | Idle workers shall be able to issue steal requests when local queues are empty and stealing is enabled. |
| CI-004 | Application-level MPI routines | Application code shall be able to invoke legacy MPI routines through SPMD execution without requiring users to manage the library's internal MPI details. |

### 2.5 Assumptions and Dependencies
| ID | Assumption or dependency |
|---|---|
| AD-001 | MPI and `mpi4py` are available for MPI-based execution. |
| AD-002 | `termcolor` is available as a main Python package dependency. |
| AD-003 | Worker availability determines achievable parallel execution. |
| AD-004 | `TORC_WORKERS` controls the number of worker threads used by each MPI process, with default value `1`. |
| AD-005 | Some callback examples assume a single worker thread per MPI process. |
| AD-006 | Image preprocessing examples require input image files organized in labeled subfolders and packages for image/HDF5 processing. |

@startuml
skinparam componentStyle rectangle
left to right direction

skinparam defaultFontName Arial
skinparam shadowing false
skinparam ArrowColor #333333
skinparam ArrowThickness 1.5
skinparam package {
    BackgroundColor #FFFFFF
    BorderColor #777777
    BorderThickness 1
}
skinparam rectangle {
    BackgroundColor #FFFFFF
    BorderColor #555555
    BorderThickness 1
}

rectangle "MPI" as MpiRuntime

package "Compute node" as ComputeNode1 {
    rectangle "MPI Process" as MpiProcess1 {
        rectangle "Worker\nThread" as WorkerThread1
        rectangle "Task Queue" as TaskQueue1
        rectangle "Server\nThread" as ServerThread1
    }
}

package "Compute node" as ComputeNode2 {
    rectangle "MPI Process" as MpiProcess2 {
        rectangle "Worker\nThread" as WorkerThread2
        rectangle "Task Queue" as TaskQueue2
        rectangle "Server\nThread" as ServerThread2
    }
}

package "Task Queue (in detail)" as TaskQueueDetail {
    rectangle "Level 0" as Level0
    rectangle "Level 1" as Level1
    rectangle "Level 2" as Level2
    rectangle "Level 3" as Level3
    rectangle "Level 4" as Level4
    rectangle "Level 5" as Level5
    rectangle "..." as MoreLevels
    rectangle "A1" as TaskA1
    rectangle "A2" as TaskA2
    rectangle "A3" as TaskA3
    rectangle "B1" as TaskB1
    rectangle "B2" as TaskB2
}

MpiRuntime <--> MpiProcess1 : MPI communication
MpiRuntime <--> MpiProcess2 : MPI communication
WorkerThread1 --> TaskQueue1 : pulls tasks
ServerThread1 --> TaskQueue1 : handles task requests
WorkerThread2 --> TaskQueue2 : pulls tasks
ServerThread2 --> TaskQueue2 : handles task requests
TaskQueue1 ..> TaskQueueDetail : expanded view
TaskQueue2 ..> TaskQueueDetail : same queue structure
Level0 --> TaskA1
TaskA1 --> TaskA2
TaskA2 --> TaskA3
Level1 --> TaskB1
TaskB1 --> TaskB2
Level0 -[hidden]down-> Level1
Level1 -[hidden]down-> Level2
Level2 -[hidden]down-> Level3
Level3 -[hidden]down-> Level4
Level4 -[hidden]down-> Level5
Level5 ..> MoreLevels : more levels
@enduml

project_context.introduction:
### 1.1 Purpose

This Software Requirements Specification defines the checked requirements for a Google Kubernetes Engine deployment that distributes Google Cloud Pub/Sub topic messages to web clients over WebSockets.

The document is prepared for architecture view generation and omits review notes, provenance fields, and supporting-material identifiers.

### 1.2 Product Scope

The product is a WebSocket adapter and deployment package that:

- Mirrors messages from a configured Google Cloud Pub/Sub topic to connected WebSocket clients.
- Lets web clients receive topic data without directly using the Pub/Sub API or SDK.
- Runs as a containerized runtime in Google Kubernetes Engine.
- Exposes the adapter through a Kubernetes `LoadBalancer` service.
- Uses a Kubernetes ConfigMap value to bind a deployment instance to one Pub/Sub topic.
- Uses a `websocketd` bridge with a Node.js `pulltop` process to serve topic data to clients.
- Supports horizontal pod autoscaling through Kubernetes autoscaling configuration.

Each deployment instance binds one externally reachable WebSocket endpoint to one configured Pub/Sub topic. Multiple topics shall be served by multiple independently configured deployment instances.

### 1.3 Intended Audience

The intended audience includes deployment operators, Kubernetes operators, GCP project administrators, web-client integrators, testers, and architecture evaluators.

### 1.4 Definitions and Abbreviations

- GCP: Google Cloud Platform.
- GKE: Google Kubernetes Engine.
- HPA: Horizontal Pod Autoscaler.
- Pub/Sub: Google Cloud Pub/Sub.
- SRS: Software Requirements Specification.
- VM: Virtual machine.
- WebSocket: Full-duplex network communication protocol used by connected clients.
project_context.asr:
## 6. Non-Functional Requirements

### NFR-001 Horizontal Scalability

The deployment shall support horizontal pod scaling through Kubernetes HPA configuration.

**Metric:** The HPA targets the adapter Deployment with min replicas `1`, max replicas `2`, and CPU utilization target `80`.

### NFR-002 Subscription Efficiency

The adapter shall support many WebSocket clients while using one Pub/Sub subscription per running VM or pod instance.

**Metric:** For `N` running instances and `M` connected clients, subscription consumption scales with `N` rather than `M`.

### NFR-003 Client Data Continuity

The adapter shall provide recent cached messages so client displays can show meaningful data during low-traffic periods.

**Metric:** A client can receive up to the last 10 cached messages for the configured topic.

### NFR-004 Deployment Compatibility

The adapter runtime shall be deployable as a container exposing port `8080` behind a Kubernetes `LoadBalancer` service exposing TCP port `80`.

**Metric:** The running service maps TCP `80` to adapter port `8080`.

### NFR-005 Client Simplicity

Web clients shall receive mirrored Pub/Sub topic data through WebSockets without direct Pub/Sub API or SDK integration.

**Metric:** Client integration requires the WebSocket endpoint and does not require Pub/Sub client credentials.

## 7. System Constraints

### CON-001 GCP Project Constraint

Deployment requires a GCP project with billing enabled.

### CON-002 Topic Access Constraint

Deployment requires read access to the configured Pub/Sub topic. The topic may be in another project if the deployment identity can read it.

### CON-003 Subscription Permission Constraint

Deployment requires permission to create Pub/Sub subscriptions.

### CON-004 GKE Deployment Constraint

The adapter shall run in Google Kubernetes Engine.

### CON-005 LoadBalancer Service Constraint

External access shall be provided through a Kubernetes `LoadBalancer` service using TCP port `80` and adapter port `8080`.

### CON-006 Runtime Composition Constraint

The checked runtime architecture shall include `pulltop`, `exec.sh`, and `websocketd` as runtime elements.

### CON-007 Topic Configuration Constraint

The configured topic value shall be passed from Kubernetes configuration into the adapter runtime through the `SYMBOL` environment variable.

### CON-008 Autoscaling Metric Constraint

Connected-client-count autoscaling shall not be assumed unless the deployment includes an explicit metric path for connected client count.

### 2.4 Operating Environment

The product shall operate in a GCP project with billing enabled and a GKE cluster available. It shall run as a containerized workload exposed through a Kubernetes `LoadBalancer` service using TCP port `80` and adapter port `8080`.

The deployment shall include a Kubernetes ConfigMap for the topic identifier, a Kubernetes Deployment for adapter pods, a Kubernetes Service for external access, and an HPA for horizontal scaling of the Deployment.

### 2.5 Assumptions and Dependencies

- The configured Pub/Sub topic is readable by the deploying user or service account.
- The deployment identity can create Pub/Sub subscriptions.
- The configured topic value is injected into the runtime as the `SYMBOL` environment variable.
- Web clients can establish WebSocket connections to the exposed endpoint.
- Horizontal scaling by connected-client count is not assumed unless an explicit connection-count metric path is configured.

project_context.introduction:
### Purpose
This SRS defines checked software requirements for TraceLeft. The specification is limited to repository-supported behavior for the TraceLeft library, probe-loading and event-delivery model, configuration-driven handler generation, and the reference CLI used to trace Linux processes.

### Product scope
TraceLeft is a Linux tracing framework composed of a reusable library and a reference command-line tool. It traces applications by installing Linux eBPF probes on function calls and delivering syscall, file, and network events for traced processes. The repository also supports configuration-driven generation of eBPF handler source code from event configuration metadata.

### Intended audience
This document is intended for:
- Maintainers of the TraceLeft library and CLI
- Architects deriving runtime structure and interfaces
- Integrators building tracing or auditing tools on TraceLeft
- Test engineers validating observable behavior
project_context.asr:
## 5. Non-Functional Requirements

### NFR-001 Platform targeting
TraceLeft shall target Linux environments with kernel support for eBPF, `kprobe`, and `kretprobe`. The documented tested environment is Linux kernel v4.11+ with such support.

Verification method: Inspection  
Acceptance criteria:
- Product documentation identifies Linux with eBPF, `kprobe`, and `kretprobe` support as the target environment.
- The documented tested environment includes Linux kernel v4.11+ with the required probe support.

### NFR-002 Event interoperability
All emitted events shall start with the common event section required by the userspace tracer for dispatch.

Verification method: Test  
Acceptance criteria:
- Emitted event records contain the common leading section.
- Event consumers can dispatch records based on that section.

### NFR-003 Technology compatibility
The implementation shall remain compatible with the `gobpf`-based integration used by the product.

Verification method: Inspection  
Acceptance criteria:
- Repository implementation uses `gobpf` as the eBPF integration technology.
- Interfaces and build assumptions remain consistent with that dependency.

### NFR-004 Configurability
The system shall support configuration-driven generation of tracing handlers from structured event metadata rather than requiring per-handler hardcoding.

Verification method: Test  
Acceptance criteria:
- Structured event metadata can be supplied to the generator.
- Handler source generation reflects the supplied configuration.

## 7. System Constraints

### C-001 Platform constraint
The product is constrained to Linux target environments.

### C-002 Kernel capability constraint
The product is constrained by the availability of kernel support for eBPF, `kprobe`, and `kretprobe`.

### C-003 Technology constraint
The implementation is constrained to the `gobpf`-based integration used by the product.

### C-004 Handler naming constraint
Handler integration is constrained by the documented naming rules:
- Handler maps use `handle_NAME_progs` and `handle_NAME_progs_ret`
- Handler probes use `kprobe/handle_NAME` and `kretprobe/handle_NAME`

### Operating environment
- Linux environments
- Linux kernels with support for eBPF, `kprobe`, and `kretprobe`
- Userspace environments capable of running the TraceLeft CLI and consuming tracer events

### Communication interfaces
- Kernel-space handler programs shall deliver events to the userspace tracer through a perf-based event channel.
- Emitted events shall contain a common leading section so the userspace tracer can dispatch them.

### Assumptions and dependencies
- TraceLeft depends on Linux eBPF facilities and probe support in the target kernel.
- The implementation depends on `gobpf`.
- Handler integration depends on documented naming rules for handler maps and handler probes.
- Configuration-driven generation depends on event configuration metadata being available in the expected schema.

samples.gt_source:
@startuml
skinparam componentStyle rectangle
left to right direction

skinparam defaultFontName Arial
skinparam shadowing false
skinparam ArrowColor #34495E
skinparam ArrowThickness 1.5
skinparam package {
    BackgroundColor #FFFFFF
    BorderColor #555555
    BorderThickness 1
}
skinparam rectangle {
    BackgroundColor #FFFFFF
    BorderColor #555555
    BorderThickness 1
}
skinparam database {
    BackgroundColor #FFFFFF
    BorderColor #555555
    BorderThickness 1
}

package "Linux Kernel Functions" as KernelFunctions {
    rectangle "sys_write()" as SysWrite
    rectangle "sys_open()" as SysOpen
    rectangle "inet_csk_accept()" as InetAccept
}

package "Main eBPF Program" as MainEbpf {
    rectangle "Kprobe\nentry probe" as Kprobe
    rectangle "Kretprobe\nreturn probe" as Kretprobe
    rectangle "Kprobe / Kretprobe\nother traced calls" as OtherProbe
    database "Prog Map\npid to fd" as ProgMap
    database "Ret Prog Map\npid to fd" as RetProgMap
    database "Prog Maps\nhandler mappings" as OtherProgMaps
}

database "Prog ID Map\npid to id" as ProgIdMap

package "Event Handler eBPF Programs" as HandlerPrograms {
    rectangle "Handler Probe\nhandle_syscall_write" as WriteHandler
    rectangle "Default Handler Probes" as DefaultHandlers
    rectangle "Process Specific\nHandler Probes" as ProcessHandlers
}

database "Perf Event Map\nshared event map" as PerfEventMap

rectangle "TraceLeft\nCLI and library tracer" as TraceLeft

SysWrite --> Kprobe : triggers entry probe
SysWrite --> Kretprobe : triggers return probe
SysOpen --> OtherProbe : triggers trace probe
InetAccept --> OtherProbe : triggers network trace

Kprobe --> ProgMap : lookup handler fd
Kretprobe --> RetProgMap : lookup return handler fd
OtherProbe --> OtherProgMaps : lookup handler mapping

TraceLeft --> ProgMap : update default handlers
TraceLeft --> RetProgMap : update return handlers
TraceLeft --> OtherProgMaps : update process handlers
TraceLeft --> ProgIdMap : update pid to program id

ProgMap --> WriteHandler : tail call
RetProgMap --> WriteHandler : tail call return handler
OtherProgMaps --> DefaultHandlers : tail call
OtherProgMaps --> ProcessHandlers : tail call
WriteHandler --> ProgIdMap : lookup program id

WriteHandler --> PerfEventMap : perf_event_output\ncommon section first
DefaultHandlers --> PerfEventMap : emit events\ncommon section first
ProcessHandlers --> PerfEventMap : emit events\ncommon section first
PerfEventMap --> TraceLeft : events
@enduml

project_context.introduction:
### Purpose
This SRS defines the checked software requirements for the `FAC10/week4-jajascript` web application. The specification is limited to behavior and interfaces supported by the reviewed repository artifacts.

### Product scope
The product is a Nobel Prize laureates autocomplete web application. A user enters text into an input field and receives Nobel Prize laureate suggestions to support easier search. The application serves a home page, handles search requests separately from other requests, and serves static web assets.

### Intended audience
This document is intended for developers, testers, maintainers, and reviewers of the application.

### References
- Repository: `FAC10/week4-jajascript`
- Reviewed artifacts include project documentation, routing and handler modules, autocomplete/search algorithm code, frontend and backend tests, and the architecture diagram.
project_context.asr:
## 5. Non-Functional Requirements

| ID | Quality attribute | Requirement | Priority | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NFR-001 | Maintainability | The project shall include automated tests covering frontend helper behavior and backend autocomplete/data behavior. | Medium | Inspection |
| NFR-002 | Compatibility | For supported static file types, the server shall identify response media type explicitly as `text/html`, `text/css`, `application/javascript`, `image/jpg`, or `image/x-icon`. | Medium | Test, Inspection |
| NFR-003 | Modularity | The application shall separate frontend input/request handling, backend routing, request handling, search algorithm, and local data access into distinct responsibility modules. | Medium | Inspection |
| NFR-004 | Deployability | The application shall support runtime startup through the Node.js start script and shall be suitable for Heroku deployment as stated in project documentation. | Low | Inspection, Demonstration |

## 7. System Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
|---|---|
| C-001 | Server-side implementation is constrained to a JavaScript/CommonJS environment using module import/export patterns compatible with Node.js. |
| C-002 | Static content serving is constrained to filesystem reads from the public asset directory. |
| C-003 | Explicitly mapped static file extensions are limited to `html`, `css`, `js`, `jpg`, and `ico`. |
| C-004 | Search routing is implemented by URL substring matching; any URL containing the substring `search` is routed to autocomplete logic. |
| C-005 | The application is expected to be deployed to Heroku and started using the Node.js start script. |

### Operating environment
| Aspect | Description |
|---|---|
| Server runtime | JavaScript server environment using CommonJS modules |
| Client environment | Web browser consuming HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and image assets |
| Data environment | Local JSON-based laureate data used by autocomplete logic |
| Deployment environment | The application is expected to run using the Node.js start script and is intended for deployment to Heroku |

### Software/API interfaces
| Interface | Requirement summary |
|---|---|
| `GET /` | The system shall serve the application home page. |
| URL containing `search` | The router shall delegate requests containing the substring `search` to the autocomplete/search-hint algorithm module. |
| Static file requests | The router shall delegate non-home, non-search requests to public file serving. |
| Local data source | The autocomplete/search-hint algorithm shall use local laureate data. |

### Communication interfaces
| Interface | Requirement summary |
|---|---|
| HTTP request/response | The server shall accept HTTP requests and return HTTP responses for home page, search, and static asset access. |

### Assumptions and dependencies
| ID | Statement |
|---|---|
| A-001 | The application depends on files under the public asset directory, including the home page and static assets. |
| A-002 | Backend autocomplete depends on local laureate data. |
| A-003 | Search routing depends on URL substring matching for `search`. |
| A-004 | The application is expected to be deployed to Heroku as described in project documentation, using the Node.js start script. |

@startuml
skinparam componentStyle rectangle
left to right direction

skinparam defaultFontName Arial
skinparam shadowing false
skinparam ArrowColor #333333
skinparam ArrowThickness 1.5
skinparam package {
    BackgroundColor #FFD4D8
    BorderColor #555555
    BorderThickness 1
}
skinparam rectangle {
    BackgroundColor #FFFFCC
    BorderColor #333333
    BorderThickness 1
}
skinparam database {
    BackgroundColor #E0FFD0
    BorderColor #333333
    BorderThickness 1
}

package "FRONTEND" as Frontend {
    rectangle "userInput" as UserInput
    rectangle "XMLHttpRequest" as XmlHttpRequest
    rectangle "index.html" as IndexHtml
    rectangle "main.css" as MainCss
}

package "BACKEND" as Backend {
    rectangle "Router" as Router
    rectangle "handlers" as Handlers
    rectangle "algorithm" as Algorithm
    database "public assets\nhtml\ncss\njs" as StaticAssets
    database "data.json" as DataJson
}

UserInput --> XmlHttpRequest : search text
IndexHtml --> UserInput : input field
IndexHtml --> MainCss : loads stylesheet
XmlHttpRequest --> Router : search request
Router --> Algorithm : search route\nserveHints
Algorithm --> DataJson : laureate data
Algorithm --> XmlHttpRequest : suggestions response
Router --> Handlers : "/" or public asset request
Handlers --> StaticAssets : read public files
Handlers --> XmlHttpRequest : html/css/js response
@enduml

project_context.introduction:
### Purpose
This Software Requirements Specification defines the checked requirements for `kubescape/host-scanner`. It captures the product behavior, interfaces, constraints, and verification criteria supported by the reviewed material for this sample.

### Product scope
`host-scanner` is a host information data collection component in the Kubescape project. It collects Kubernetes node host information to support later security posture assessment. It is typically deployed as a privileged Kubernetes DaemonSet and provides host information to clients through an HTTP API.

This specification includes the subset of behavior confirmed for this sample, including:
- HTTP endpoint behavior validated by end-to-end tests
- CNI configuration directory resolution from runtime and process configuration inputs
- Removal of encryption provider configuration secrets from JSON-like control-plane data
- Provider-specific end-to-end test selection using Go build tags

### Intended audience
- Maintainers of `kubescape/host-scanner`
- Architects and developers integrating with the HTTP API
- Test engineers validating endpoint, runtime-resolution, and sanitization behavior
- Operators deploying and running the component
project_context.asr:
## 5. Non-Functional Requirements

| ID | Quality attribute | Requirement | Priority | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NFR-001 | Security | The system shall not expose encryption provider configuration secrets in processed JSON output. | High | Test |
| NFR-002 | Maintainability and portability | End-to-end tests shall select provider-specific expected data structures through Go build tags. Platform-specific test files shall use the `//go:build <provider>` form to declare tags. | Medium | Inspection |
| NFR-003 | Compatibility | The HTTP API shall use standard HTTP request/response semantics, including status-code-based reporting for unknown paths. | Medium | Test |

## 7. System Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
|---|---|
| C-001 | Provider-specific end-to-end test selection is constrained to Go build-tag mechanisms using `//go:build <provider>` declarations. |
| C-002 | CNI configuration directory resolution is constrained by the available runtime type, configuration-file content, defaults, and command-line arguments. |
| C-003 | HTTP behavior verification is constrained to externally observable HTTP request/response interactions. |
| C-004 | The product is intended for Kubernetes node-host data collection and privileged DaemonSet deployment scenarios. |

### Operating environment
The system operates in the following environment:
- Kubernetes-based deployment environment
- Privileged DaemonSet deployment model
- HTTP service reachable by clients and end-to-end tests
- Go-based implementation and test environment
- Linux-style host and runtime filesystem paths, including paths under `/etc`, `/run`, and `/var/lib`
- Container runtime and node-agent contexts such as kubelet, containerd, and CRI-O scenarios

### Software interfaces
| Interface | Requirement |
|---|---|
| HTTP API | The system shall expose HTTP endpoints accessible through standard HTTP requests. |
| Runtime and process configuration input | The system shall accept runtime-specific configuration data, configuration-file content, and command-line arguments used to derive the CNI configuration directory. |
| JSON processing interface | The system shall accept control-plane JSON input and produce sanitized JSON output. |
| Provider-specific test interface | The end-to-end test suite shall support provider-specific expected data selection through Go build tags using `//go:build <provider>` declarations. |

### Communication interfaces
| Interface | Requirement |
|---|---|
| HTTP | The system shall support HTTP request/response communication for endpoint access and status-code-based responses. |

### Assumptions and dependencies
- The system depends on access to Kubernetes node host information.
- HTTP-based interactions depend on a running host-scanner service instance.
- CNI directory resolution depends on runtime type, default configuration, configuration-file content, and command-line arguments.
- Provider-specific end-to-end tests depend on Go build tags.
- Secret removal operates on JSON-formatted control-plane data.

project_context.introduction:
### Purpose
This SRS defines software requirements for the `allegroai/clearml-agent` component, focusing on the ClearML agent runtime, its Kubernetes-oriented execution flow, worker telemetry behavior, and exposed backend service interfaces.

### Product scope
The product supports:
- running ClearML jobs through Kubernetes-related integration modes,
- installing an experiment environment inside a pod and starting and monitoring the experiment process,
- periodic worker reporting and connectivity checks,
- backend service interactions for entity creation and retrieval, and
- selected events and models service data structures.

### Intended audience
This document is intended for:
- product and requirements owners,
- maintainers of `allegroai/clearml-agent`,
- integrators deploying the agent in Kubernetes environments, and
- test and validation engineers.

### References
- Repository: `allegroai/clearml-agent`
project_context.asr:
## 5. Non-Functional Requirements

| ID | Quality attribute | Requirement | Priority | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NFR-001 | Operability | The default worker telemetry configuration shall provide status reporting every 2 seconds and connectivity ping every 30 seconds unless overridden by configuration. | Medium | Inspection |
| NFR-002 | Operability | The worker shall support two memory reporting scopes: process or subprocess scope by default, and entire-machine scope when `report_global_mem_used` is enabled. | Low | Test |
| NFR-003 | Compatibility | The system shall maintain compatibility with versioned backend service interfaces including at least models service v2.4 and events service v2.5 as represented by the available request and response structures. | Medium | Inspection |

## 7. System Constraints

| ID | Constraint |
|---|---|
| C-001 | The long-lasting service-pod integration is constrained to use the `clearml-agent` Docker image. |
| C-002 | Sibling Docker container management in service-pod mode requires mapping the Docker socket into the pod. |
| C-003 | Kubernetes Glue job preparation requires a provided YAML template. |
| C-004 | Supported backend interfaces are constrained by versioned service definitions, including events v2.5 and models v2.4. |
| C-005 | Kubernetes service-pod mode currently depends on Docker-socket mapping for sibling-container management; this mechanism is planned to be replaced by podman. |

### Operating environment
- Kubernetes cluster environment with pods and Kubernetes jobs
- Docker-based execution environment, including sibling container management through a mapped Docker socket in service-pod mode
- Connectivity to a ClearML server or backend for status reporting, ping, and service requests

### Software/API interfaces
| Interface | Requirement summary |
|---|---|
| Backend service client | The system shall support backend service actions including `create`, `get`, `get_by_id`, `get_all`, and `get_all_ex` through session-based request handling. |
| Events service interface | The system shall support request and response structures for multi-task scalar metrics iteration histograms. |
| Models service interface | The system shall support model deletion request structures and model data object fields. |

### Communication interfaces
| Interface | Requirement summary |
|---|---|
| Server connectivity ping | The worker shall ping the server at a configurable period. |
| Queue-based job retrieval | Kubernetes Glue shall pull jobs from the ClearML execution queue. |

### Assumptions and dependencies
- Kubernetes Glue operation depends on a provided YAML template for Kubernetes job preparation.
- Long-lasting service-pod operation depends on the `clearml-agent` Docker image and mapped Docker socket access.
- Worker telemetry depends on connectivity to the ClearML server.
- Backend service actions depend on backend session request and response handling.
